chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the Committee on Finance, joined with Committees on Children and Youth and Immigration. Devices are silenced and dais rules announced.
chapters.title: Hearing called to order and housekeeping

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council Executive Budget Hearing for the Committee on Finance, joined with the Committee on Children and Youth, and joined with the Committee on Immigration.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all electronic devices and no one may approach the dais at any time.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Linda Lee opens day three of FY27 executive budget hearings, welcoming Commissioner Jones-Gaston and outlining ACS's proposed $3.51B budget, a 2.6% increase from preliminary. She highlights concerns about voucher waitlists, Raise the Age reimbursement, and savings methodology.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens ACS hearing with FY27 budget overview

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, wait, oh, got a gavel.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Always forget that part.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to day three of the fiscal FY27 executive budget hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee and today's hearings will begin with the Administration for Children's Services followed by Department of Youth and Community Development and the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm pleased to be joined by my colleague and dear friend, Councilmember Althea Stevens, Chair of the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And today we have been joined by Councilmember Marte, Councilmember Shulman, Councilmember Epstein, Councilmember Joseph, Councilmember Wong, and Deputy Speaker Williams is on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner Rebecca Jones-Gaston.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Week two, or day seven, rather, day six, to you and your team, and of course your amazing team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for fiscal 2026 to 2027.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 30, with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Administration for Children's Services proposed fiscal FY27 budget of $3.51 billion represents

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 2.7% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan, and this is an increase of $89.6 million, or 2.6%,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: from the $3.42 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, including additional funding added for child care vouchers, foster care, staffing across the agency, and a baselining of the Promise NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 2026, ACS's headcount was 562 less than their FY26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to apply for the state waiver that would allow the City to apply for reimbursement of its expenditures across fiscal 2019 to 2026

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: related to the implementation of the State's Raise the Age Act.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further in our response, we called on the administration to continue to invest in child care vouchers by baselining the Promise NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In our preliminary budget hearing, we encouraged the administration to explore ways to end the waiting list practice for child care vouchers, which now stands at approximately 25,000 children.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: While we were pleased to see the Promise New York City baselined and some additional funding added for child care vouchers, I hope to see

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: progress made in reducing the voucher wait list and ensuring these families are able to receive the vital child care services that they need, whether through ACS or other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing from our new commissioner on how ACS plans to address the urgent challenge of providing child care and continue to equitably serve families amid citywide savings requirements.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My questions today will largely focus on areas in which ACS was able to realize savings, the agency's process and methodology in identifying these savings, and updates on the nearly $200 million in state funding designated for at-risk youth.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Althea Stevens delivers her opening statement emphasizing that the city must be affordable for young people. She raises concerns about savings targets hitting ACS, the $194.5M in state at-risk youth funding, and the Raise the Age hardship waiver application.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens opening statement on ACS budget and youth priorities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Councilmember Stevens, for her opening statement.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Good morning and thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: My name is Althea Stevens and I'm serves as the chair of Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: For this first part of our hearing this morning, I look forward to discussing changes to ACS budgets since the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The executive budget included investments in several areas, including child care vouchers, detention facilities, and staffing across the agency.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: New needs of $55.7 million were added to the fiscal 2026 in the executive plan, growing to $104.6 million in fiscal 2027 and dropping to $80.7 million in fiscal 2028 and in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Additionally, other adjustments in fiscal 2026 totaled $547.6 million, dropping to $2 million in fiscal 2027 and in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: While the city's budget now has been balanced without property taxes increased, all the agencies were required to meet savings and vacancy reduction benchmarks.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: As agencies providing frontline services to some of the city's most vulnerable residents, understanding the impact of these savings targeted ACS is critical.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: My theme for today, and will always continue to be, the city will not be considered affordable if it's not affordable for young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because if we don't invest in our young people on the front end, we then ultimately invest in them on the back end when they are living and doing things that are not things that we want to see.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I look forward to discussing how ACS responded to the mayor's savings requirements, their methodology, the rationale, and identifying areas where savings could be made.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: could be realized, and most critically, the effects on services or programming that these savings might have.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm also interested in understanding how the agency is working with the state partners to maximize the funding in vital services such as child care vouchers, preventative services, and programming.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm also really curious to hear about the plan for the money that was allocated from the governor because in our

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: preliminary hearing, that plan still had not been discussed with us and want to make sure that the money that was allocated for youth services and the raise the age that we are thinking about and also would like to hear if we're going to

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: put in a hardship application.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In the Council's responsibility to ensure that the City budget is fair, transparent, and accountable to New Yorkers, as the Chair of Children and Youth Committee, I will continue to push for accountability and transparency in the ACS budget.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Amidst the needs to eliminate budget gaps and meet saving requirements, our responsibility is in acting a budget that reflects the needs and interests of our City's youth, taking on even higher importance.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like the Mayor has often repeated, this budget must not be balanced on the back of working people or young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm adding the or because nobody keeps talking about the young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So today, through and throughout the examination of AC budget, we ensure that this is the case.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Before we get started, I would like to take a quick moment to thank both my staff and the entire council fiscal division for their efforts in preparing for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Margaret, Julia,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Richard, Ria, Elizabeth, and my Deputy Chief of Staff, Greer, and the entire A-Team back in the Suite 16.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: At this time, I'll pass it back to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee thanks the Council Finance team and announces that all public testimony will be held Wednesday, June 10th at 9:30 AM. She then turns to swearing in witnesses.
chapters.title: Chair Lee thanks staff and announces public testimony date

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before we get started, just wanted to take a moment to thank the entire Council Finance team and Division for preparing today's hearing, especially Julia Haramis and Margaret Barnsley

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: for today's hearing, and of course, Brian Sarfo and all the finance analysts and support staff who make it all work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just as a reminder, for this year's executive budget hearings, because we're on a tighter timeline than we normally are, we're going to be doing all of the public testimony on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I see some of our partners here from our nonprofits that are here with us, which I'm glad, actually, because I think it's always important to be in the room and listen to the feedback.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also, hopefully, it'll help inform the questions you do have when we hopefully will see you back on the 10th, June 10th, Wednesday, starting at 9.30 a.m.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Rebecca Jones-Gaston and six deputy commissioners before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Swearing in of ACS commissioner and deputy commissioners

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I will turn it now over to our committee counsel to swear in the witnesses for their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Commissioner Jones-Gaston?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Pletnikoff?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Gendel?

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Gendell
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Mendez?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Linares?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Welcomer?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Deputy Commissioner Ginsberg?

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: New Commissioner Jones-Gaston shares her personal connection to child welfare, her career from frontline practice to federal leadership, and her commitment to equitable, prevention-driven, family-centered systems change at ACS.
chapters.title: Commissioner Jones-Gaston: personal background and vision for ACS

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: My name is Rebecca Jones-Gaston, and I am honored to serve as the Commissioner for the Administration of Children's Services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'd like to thank Chair Stevens, Chair Lee, and the members of the Children and Youth and Finance Committees for convening today's hearing on the Executive Budget for City Fiscal Year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am joined by my colleagues, Deputy Commissioner for Financial Services, Margaret Plentikoff, Deputy Commissioner for External Affairs, Stephanie Glendell,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner for the Division of Youth and Family Justice, Nancy Ginsberg, Deputy Commissioner for the Family Services Division, Louisa Linares,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner for Family Permanency Services, Ina Mendez, and Deputy Commissioner for the Division of Child and Family Well-Being, Elizabeth Volcomore.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to acknowledge Melissa Hester for her steady and compassionate leadership as interim commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: During a period of transition, Melissa guided this agency with care, integrity, and deep commitment to children and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I want to thank her and congratulate her on her new role as Executive Deputy Commissioner of People, Operations, and Technology.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I often say that I was born into the

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Child welfare is not simply the work I do, it is personal to me.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It has shaped my life, my sense of purpose, and my understanding of what children, youth, and families need not only to survive, but to heal, grow, and thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Because I know personally what systems can mean in the life of a child, I approach this role with both urgency and hope.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: My professional journey has taken me from the frontline practice in child protection and foster care to leadership roles at the state and federal levels, including serving as commissioner

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: of the Administration on Children, Youth, and Families during the Biden-Harris administration.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Across every role,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Every system and every community, one lesson has remained constant.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Meaningful and lasting change only happens when we remain grounded in the voices, experiences, strengths, and hopes of the people most impacted by our decisions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That belief will guide my leadership at ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also recognize that this work does not happen in isolation.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The well-being of children and families depends on strong partnership across systems and sectors, including providers, advocates, community leaders, and our partners in the judiciary.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Lasting progress requires all of us working together with shared accountability and shared commitment to safety, healing, fairness, and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe deeply.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: that safety and connection must coexist.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children should be safe, and families should have the support they need to remain together whenever possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Communities should be seen not simply through the lens of need, but through the lens of strength, wisdom, and possibility.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children are not cases.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Families are not problems to solve.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Communities are not deficits to manage.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: They are people with stories, strengths, hopes, and potential.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And our responsibility is to shape systems that recognize humanity and respond with accountability, compassion, and intention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As Commissioner, I'm committed to continuing the work of strengthening child welfare system that is more responsive, more equitable, more preventive, and more humane.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That means ensuring child protective intention is focused on where children are truly unsafe.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: While continuing to invest in the community-based and family-centered supports that help families thrive before crisis occurs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It means listening closely to young people and families with lived experiences and recognizing them as partners in shaping the solutions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And it means continuing to transform how we support young people involved in the juvenile justice system, seeing them not only for the worst moment in their lives, but for the worst moment in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In their lives, but for the worst moment in their lives, but for the worst moment in their lives,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: their potential, resilience, and future.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also want to be clear about the tone and the spirit I hope to bring to this role.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe in stewardship, I believe in partnership, I believe in accountability rooted in compassion, and I believe in systems change

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: happens when we are willing to listen, to learn, and to act with informed intentionality.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also believe none of this work is possible without a skilled, supported, and well-resourced workforce.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Every day, ACF staff provide our partners, attorneys,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Clinicians, case planners, direct care staff, and so many others carry enormous responsibility on behalf of children and families, often under incredibly complex circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: If we want stronger outcomes for children and for families, we must continue investing in the people who make these outcomes possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I look forward to building an honest and constructive partnership with this council as we work together on behalf of New York City's children and families.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston outlines the $3.51B FY27 executive budget and credits Chief Savings Officer Margaret Pletnikoff for identifying efficiencies while minimizing impacts on children, youth, and families.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: FY27 budget overview and savings approach

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Today, I'll provide an overview of the FY 2027 executive budget, which is projected to be $3.51 billion, including $1.4 billion in city funds.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you know, the Mondani administration asked each agency to identify a chief savings officer to carefully examine opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: for savings and efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I want to thank ACS's Chief Savings Officer, Margaret Plentikoff, for leading this work thoughtfully, with continued focus on minimizing impacts on children, families, and providers who depend on our services.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston highlights state-city budget agreements enabling continued voucher recertification, support for HRA cash assistance families, and the baselining of Promise NYC at $25M for approximately 1,000 child care slots.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: child care vouchers and Promise NYC baselined

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Affordable child care is essential for families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I am extremely pleased that the Momdani administration has prioritized expanding across the quality child care throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS's budget furthers the commitment in a number of ways.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Due to the advocacy from the Mamdani administration, the City Council, and many others, the state and city have come to a number of budgetary agreements that will enable New York City to expand and maintain access to early childhood education.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As it relates to ACS,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The funding from the city and the state in ACS's budget will enable us to continue to recertify the vast majority of families receiving low-income child care vouchers from ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The funding will also allow the city to support the growing need for child care vouchers among families on cash assistance who are served by the Human Resources Administration.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we are extremely pleased to share that the FY27 executive budget restores and baselines Promise NYC at $25 million.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding will enable ACS to maintain city-funded, community-based child care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: supports approximately 1,000 slots.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Historically, Promise NYC was funded for one year at a time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Baselining the funding for this crucial program creates certainty and stability.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I have dedicated my career to identifying ways to support and uplift families and communities so that they can be safe and thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As I take on this role, I remain committed to lifting up family-centered, prevention-driven solutions that improve outcomes and transform how systems support children, families, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am committed to keeping children safe by providing ACS child protective staff with the tools, supervision, and training they need to make sound decisions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I am committed to ensuring ACS helps to provide families and communities with the support they need to succeed.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston announces 11 community partnership providers, details the prevention services network of 44 providers and 10,277 slots, and notes the savings from closing four prevention programs totaling 360 slots for $2.7M CTL savings.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: community partnerships and prevention services

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS will soon announce the providers recommended for the 11 community partnerships across the city.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Community partnerships are neighborhood coalitions that enhance child and family well-being through strategies responsive to each community's specific wants and needs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: By listening to the voices and ideas of community members, the community partnerships work to increase coordination among service providers, connect families to resources, and fill programming gaps.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The new contracts will expand the role of community ambassadors, trusted community residents who build relationships with and connect families

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: to information and resources.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm looking forward to the implementation of these new contracts and the positive impact the partnerships will continue to have.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS currently contracts with 44 provider agencies operating 127 programs for family services, previously referred to as prevention services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Across the system, ACS contracts for 10,277 slots, meaning more than 10,000 families can receive services at any one point in time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In calendar year 2025, a little over 30,000 children in 14,000 families received child welfare, family support, and therapeutic treatment services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS and our providers have been intentional about supporting communities, engaging families, and connecting them to resources.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Through this work, we've increased community referrals from 12% of all families served in January 2023 to 27% of them in March of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: These include referrals from schools, healthcare providers, and families themselves.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As part of our efforts to look closely at our budget and possible efficiencies, this budget includes closing four programs totaling 360 slots for savings of $2.7 million

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: CTL and $7.1 million gross.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We will be working closely with the four providers as well.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: as the providers in the same catchment area to ensure that no family loses services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We will also continue to monitor community needs and make sure families continue to have access to services.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston reports fewer than 6,300 children in foster care, down from 42,000 when ACS was created. She explains budget reductions from underspending surpluses in foster care codes with no impact on providers or youth.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: foster care at historic lows and budget reductions

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In New York City, the number of children in foster care is at a historic low, with fewer than 6,300 children in care, down from 13,000 a decade ago and close to 42,000 when ACS was created 30 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Our work at ACS centers on continuing to reduce the number of children in care, both by preventing children from entering in the first place and from intensifying efforts to reunify children more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am committed to working with the ACS team and our providers to continue this reduction.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: For those children and youth in our care, it is essential that we provide them with the resources and skills that they need to become successful and thriving New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This budget includes important actions that will enable us to do this.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes a number of budgetary reductions in the foster care area that will have no impact on children, youth, families, or providers,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: as the savings are from underspending or surpluses in specific budget codes, which I'll explain in more detail.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston details the Youth Safety and Success Initiative investments, including 470 youth in College Choice (up from 430) and the launch of Career Choice. Budget adjustments slow the Fair Futures ramp-up while maintaining full ramp-up by FY29.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: Fair Futures and Career Choice program updates

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you recall, last fiscal year, ACS's budget included the Youth Safety and Success Initiative, which was a $40 million investment in programs for youth in foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: and the justice system.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically, as it relates to foster care, the initiative funded an expansion of the number of youth who could be served by Fair Futures coaches and tutors,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: the number of youth participating in the College Choice program, and the creation of a new program, Career Choice.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As a result of this investment, we see more youth getting connected to Fair Futures coaches, and there are over 470 youth enrolled in College Choice, up from 430 last year.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we've created and launched Career Choice, a new program that builds off the success of College Choice

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: by connecting young people in foster care to similar financial, social, and academic support when they elect to attend a vocational program or a job readiness program instead of college.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The program was launched in October, and the first 10 youth are now enrolled in Career Choice programming.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we are on track for 136 youth to participate in the Department of Youth and Community Development's Advance and Earn program this fiscal year, which is also a part of Career Choice.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget better aligns anticipated spending levels of both Fair Futures and Career Choice with no impact on services to better match programmatic ramp up and allocated funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically for Fair Futures, we plan for the program to grow in budget and use

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: served in both FY27 and 28, while saving about 2 million CTL in FY26 and 27 and 1.1 million in FY28.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We still anticipate full ramp up to 42.7 million and 54 youth by FY29.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Similarly, there is a 405,000 CTL and 1.1 million gross reduction in career choice in the current fiscal year 2026 due to the savings from the initial startup time needed for the program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: All residential and specialized family foster care providers receive $3,500 for each youth ages 14 to 21 in their care for independent living services, referred to as preparing youth for adulthood.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: These services help youth develop life skills through workshops and programs such as financial literacy, resume writing, college applications, grocery shopping, banking, and driver's education.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes an annual reduction of 1.4 million CTL, 3.75 million gross, due to a surplus in the budget code.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There will be no impact on providers or youth.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Providers will continue to receive the $3,500 for each youth age 14 to 21.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes 233,000 CTL, 1.1 million gross.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: reduction for adoption recruitment contracts, which ACS no longer maintains and has not since 2015.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Adoption and foster parent recruitment is now managed by our foster care providers and their current contracts include recruitment funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thus, there is no interruption in service and we do not anticipate any impact on providers or families.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston explains the $97.5M CTL to address the Title IV-E foster care reimbursement gap caused by outdated AFDC income eligibility. She also highlights $1.6M for immigration legal services and fees for youth in foster care.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: Title IV-E gap and immigration legal services

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes $97.5 million in CTL funding to address the Title IV-E budget gap the ACS has been carrying due to the way the federal government reimburses for foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically, while federal Title IV-E funding for foster care is a federal entitlement, it is only an entitlement for those children and youth who are federally eligible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: While there are a number of eligibility requirements, family income is the one that causes most children to be ineligible for federal reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: To be eligible, current income of the child's family must be such that they would have been eligible for AFDC, which was the predecessor of TANF, in 1996.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This has never been adjusted to inflation, nor the current public assistance program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There was a period of time where the gap was being filled by federal government funds through the 4E waiver, but that ended in 2018.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This addition

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This additional funding fills the baselines, the gap ACS has been carrying.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes $1.6 million for immigration legal services and fees for children and youth in foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Since at least 2006, ACS has been working with legal service providers to assist youth in care by obtaining special immigrant juvenile status.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS is extremely grateful to these providers who have helped countless youth in care get green cards all pro bono.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Given the increasing demands for immigration legal assistance, ACS will now be able to provide attorneys handling these matters with $2,000 per child.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The Trump administration recently created new fees and increased existing fees for various immigration relief applications, and this additional funding will provide reimbursement for those fees.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston reports 362 youth in secure detention, 66 in non-secure, and 128 in close-to-home. The budget includes $6.3M for Horizon exercise pavilion, facility operations, and conversion of a 6-bed to 9-bed non-secure placement facility.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: juvenile justice facility funding and capital needs

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS continues to focus on meeting the needs of the increased number of youth across our juvenile justice continuum.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As of May 13, 2026,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There were 362 youth in secure detention, 66 youth in non-secure detention, and 128 youth in close to home.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes funding to support operations, maintenance, and capacity needs at our facilities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The budget includes $6.3 million baselined for juvenile justice facility needs associated with the creation of the exercise pavilion at Horizon,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: including the additional funding needed for maintenance repairs and operations of the existing space.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding will also be used for additional supplies needed for the population of youth in detention, such as uniforms, toiletries, bedding, and food services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Finally, a portion of this initiative is additional funding needed to support the conversion of a six-bed non-secure placement facility to a nine-bed non-secure placement facility

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: to help with the increased close-to-home census.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes additional capital needs for our juvenile justice programs, including funding for the Horizon Annex, HVACs at Horizon and

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Crossroads, as well as our other repairs and IT equipment at Horizon and Crossroads.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston closes by emphasizing the need for partnership across government, providers, and communities, and commits to shaping systems grounded in equity, accountability, and compassion amid federal policy uncertainty.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: closing remarks on partnership and transformation

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So in closing,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe this executive budget reflects an important investment in children, youth, and families and communities across New York City,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: and it represents an opportunity to continue shaping systems that are not only effective, but also compassionate, responsive, and grounded in partnership.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As I begin in this role,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I do so with humility, urgency, and hope.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I know the responsibility that comes with

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: with leading this agency, and I know the profound impact our decisions have on the lives of children and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also know this work cannot be done by any one agency or system alone.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It requires partnership across government with providers,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: advocates, communities, and with our partners in the judiciary, and most importantly, with young people and families whose lived experience must continue to inform and shape our work.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: At the same time, we are navigating a period of significant change and uncertainty nationally.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Federal policy shifts and broader pressures on social service systems continue to impact child welfare and youth serving systems across the country.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But even as systems adapt, transform, and respond to those pressures, our responsibility must remain clear.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children, youth, and families must be at the center of every decision we make.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This moment calls not only for responsiveness, but for intentional transformation rooted in equity,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: community partnership, accountability, and the belief that every child and family deserves dignity, support, stability, and possibility.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe deeply in the power of connection and intentional action.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe children, youth, and families deserve systems that see their humanity,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: honor their strengths, and create real pathways to healing, stability, opportunity, and belonging.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I believe our collective responsibility is not simply to respond to crisis, but to help create conditions where children and families and communities can truly thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That's the work ahead of us, and I look forward to doing it in partnership with all of you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you, and I welcome your questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before I go into my questions, just want to recognize we've been joined by Councilmember Marano on Zoom, Councilmembers Osei, Councilmember Felder, Councilmember Jay Sanchez, and Councilmember Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have two former nonprofit leaders up here, which really greatly appreciate the work and the portfolio that ACS has.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have two former nonprofit leaders up here, which really greatly appreciate the work and the portfolio that ACS has.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know that you are in charge of a lot, and there's a lot on your plate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know that you are in charge of a lot, and there's a lot on your plate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so one of the things I hate are silos in government.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so one of the things I hate are silos in government.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so whatever we can do on the council, we've always tried to champion ways to make sure that the agencies are also talking to each other and ways that we can play a helpful role in that as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so whatever we can do on the council, we've always tried to champion ways to make sure that the agencies are also talking to each other and ways that we can play a helpful role in that as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee

chapters.summary: Ossé raises concerns from his district about families told the city has no funding for non-mandated vouchers. Deputy Commissioner Wolkomir explains ACS continues recertifying existing vouchers but cannot issue new ones, and points to NYC Public Schools and DYCD alternatives.

chapters.summary: Stevens expresses alarm at 362 youth in secure detention, up from 150 when she took office. She demands to know about interagency efforts with DYCD, DOE, and MACJ to reduce numbers. Deputy Commissioner Ginsburg confirms active conversations with MACJ and DYCD.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about specific Fair Futures targets and how providers are informed they can request additional funding. Mendez shares targets of 4,800 for FY27 and 5,000 for FY28, and commits to ensuring contract management teams communicate funding flexibility to providers.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks what steps ACS takes to ensure providers fully utilize Fair Futures funding. Mendez describes the contract management unit's tracking of invoices, vacancies, and staff turnover, with conversations triggered when spending falls below budgeted levels.

chapters.summary: Restler confirms 25,600 children on the voucher waitlist and proposes reviving single-year SCCF vouchers to add capacity with known FY27 funding. He also raises alarm about OCFS regulations limiting vouchers to exact work hours, calling it disastrous for NYC child care.

chapters.summary: Mealy raises concerns about youth sleeping on the floor at Crossroads and questions why only 3 beds are being added. Ginsburg clarifies no one sleeps on the floor, and reveals plans for 28 additional beds at Crossroads with two new halls and classroom space.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about ACS coordination with DOC for children in foster care whose parents are incarcerated. Mendez describes the CHIP team facilitating visits and case planning, and commits to exploring a formal partnership with the new DOC commissioner.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about numbers and resources for youth aging out. Mendez reports 383 non-permanency discharges in CY2025, down from 420, and details supports including Fair Futures, CityFHEPS vouchers, Anthos aftercare, and supervision-to-21 tracking.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks whether providers have been notified in writing of prevention cuts (not yet, only verbal contact), and questions CPS vacancies. Jones-Gaston reports 191 vacancies in Child Protection, with 108 CPS positions vacant out of 1,855 budgeted.

chapters.summary: Lee welcomes Commissioner Sandra Escamilla-Davies and outlines DYCD's proposed $1.62B FY27 budget, a $14.2M decrease from preliminary. She notes savings from consulting contract elimination, vacancy reduction, and COLA adjustments, offset by CMS expansion.

utterances.text: So on May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26 through 2030 with a proposed fiscal 27 budget of $124.7 billion.

chapters.summary: Stevens praises DYCD for completing the Compass RFP after 10 years but raises concerns about providers losing contracts, RHY and peer navigator funding not baselined, and the need to protect human service workers. She urges getting all remaining RFPs done while the new commissioner is in place.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies shares her first three months listening and learning from staff, providers, and partners. She outlines her focus on examining procurement, contracting, payments, and performance management to ensure DYCD operates effectively and responsively.

utterances.text: So the criteria was organizational experience, service requirement, staffing, community partnership, approach, and budget.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Those were the criteria that we looked at, and the minimum score was a 70.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Those are the criteria that we looked at, and the minimum score was a 70.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Minimum was 70, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Minimum was 70, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know there's going to be a lot more questions on that, so I will leave it to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know there's going to be a lot more questions on that, so I will leave it to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chair Stevens?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chair Stevens?

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD is responding to providers who lost awards. Escamilla-Davies describes briefings, extended protest periods, and transition planning to support outgoing providers while maintaining dignity.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions DYCD on supporting non-awarded Compass providers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I will jump right into it and I know we are like kind of pressed for time so I just ask that the answers be succinct because we want to make sure we get to as many people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I will jump right into it, and I know we are kind of pressed for time, so I just ask that the answers be succinct, because we want to make sure we get to as many people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'll stay with the compass theme and just kind of just thinking about just the impact of like organizations and as we know these decisions aren't being made easily for you guys but just thinking about

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'll stay with the Compass theme and just kind of just thinking about just the impact of organizations, and as we know, these decisions aren't being made easily for you guys, but just thinking about...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because of this RFP, we know that some of these organizations that didn't get contracts, this is actually going to be a huge impact on their agencies.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because of this RFP, we know that some of these organizations that didn't get contracts, this is actually going to be a huge impact on their agencies.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I wanted to know how is the agencies responding?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I wanted to know how is the agencies responding?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How are you guys responding to the providers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How are you guys responding to the providers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What supports have you been giving them?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What supports have you been giving them?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What has this...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What has this transition been looking like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Especially for those we know that it's going to have big impacts where they didn't get any awards.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So we're in the protest phase for the school base, so I'm going to speak more generally.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: First, I want to say that the non-awardies, I want to acknowledge that they have financial and operational impacts that are going to force them to make really difficult decisions, and we don't take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And as I mentioned before, and this was a procurement process that followed all of the guidelines of procurement law, and yet we are going to try to stand in the gap for these providers.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're conducting briefings for them.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We've extended, based on their feedback, the protest period so that everybody can get a briefing before the date, before they send a protest letter.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But also we are trying to figure out about having conversations about what this is going to look like and if there are other things that we could do to support them.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And then really leaning into the transition periods and figuring out what does transition look like from summer to September?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: How do we help transition providers in ways that hold their dignity intact while entering new providers and making the way for good providers to also stand in that gap and support children, youth, and families?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the plan for providers running Summer Rising who will lose contracts afterward. Daniel describes coordinating closeout processes, transition convenings between CBOs, and engaging school leaders and families.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions Summer Rising transition for losing providers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And just even thinking about summer, because I know you brought up summer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So as we're thinking about summerizing, just going to record again.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Everybody knows I don't like that program.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Anyway, I just always have to say it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But anyway, wanting to think about the...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: who would be running and operating Summer Horizon and then not having a host site going back and moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What's the plan around what does ZYCD plan to support those providers who programs are closing out like right after that and and also just kind of considering what the morale for some of these programs going to going to be for this summer and closing out.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So I think, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: It's a good question because there's two parts to this, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: There is the contracting closeout process, but we want to make this process as humane as possible.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So our teams will be coordinating with those CBOs to have conversations on the process itself from the contracting side, from the financial side, and also that transition between CBO to CBO,

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: coordinating transition convenings between CBOs, connecting with school leaders and community to, again, really emphasize, like, this is not just about the contract, it's about the people on the site as well.

chapters.summary: Stevens demands details on transition toolkits, staff retention, and involving local council members. Daniel describes transition meetings, knowledge transfer, readiness checks with new CBOs, and principal-level engagement, committing to include council members.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens presses on Compass transition process and council member involvement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Could you talk to me a little bit about what the transition processes look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know that's one of my biggest things, like how are we working with organizations on this transition process and what is that going to look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You know, especially where, you know, there's cases where they might not have gotten that site, but they have another site.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so, yes, we would love for those.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Folks who work in the program to stay, but if some people are going to go to the new sites, then I just think like this reshuffling is going to cause a little bit of a chaos.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so just trying to understand like, how are we preparing?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Not only the providers, but schools and families around this transition and what it's going to look like.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Do we have like documents?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Are we like working on like toolkits?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then also how will we be involving the council members who I know a lot of them are already getting a lot of calls and pushback and want to make sure that the local council members are part of those conversations with these transition processes.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So that's a great segue because part of this process is a real coordinated effort between all those stakeholders for, again, the best

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: transition as possible because the timeline is difficult and there's a lot of concerns about incoming change, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So the first part is there's core components of the plan that focus on transition meetings between all the different stakeholders, knowledge transfer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have the transition meetings already started to take place?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Yeah, starting to take place, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: We're also keeping in mind that we are in a protest and appeal period, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And to make sure that we're coordinating all those engagements, as well as being thoughtful about them in that process

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: in a respectful way, thinking about what staff retention may look like, connecting with the school leaders,

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: family and school communication, and coordinating with New York City Public Schools centrally, and doing district-level engagement and principal-level engagement, because some instances, it's a good change that they're really anticipating for and they've been working for a long time towards.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And then what our role is really kind of that central point to lift and escalate if there are some challenging conversations along the way and what we'll be tracking along that path.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So one thing that we already started is engaging with those new CBOs and welcoming them in and really thinking about like readiness check.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Where are they as an organization?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Where are they in terms of the staffing supports, coordination, what types of activities they're looking towards doing, connecting them with the school leader.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So every single principal as part of the announcement was provided as who the CBO was and the contact information and coordinating with public schools where there are some instances where there's some disconnect.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So that's what we want to ensure throughout the.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I know it is a fast turnaround time, but again, I just want to make sure that we are tapping in the local council members to be a part of those conversations so that when we,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: so we could all be on the same page because it starts to get this conjoined when you have parents reaching out and the schools and all these things.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I want to make sure that local council members are part of the conversations and being brought in to be as supportive as possible with these transitions because I think, you know, a lot of folks are not happy.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And, you know,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We are in a place where some of these decisions are final.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so thinking about how do we work together to get not only the communities acclimated, but also the council members with some of these new organizations that will be coming into their communities.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: This is a community effort.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So all state care holders involved.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: We'll definitely be connecting with you guys.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And if there's any questions or flags, feel free to ask.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how 5,000 additional slots will be allocated and whether there will be another RFP. Escamilla-Davies says strategic decisions are underway to leverage slots for ecosystem stability, honoring the current procurement process while being opportunistic.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens asks about 5,000 additional Compass slots for FY28

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So according to the after school expansion plan, 5,000 additional campus slots will be added in fall 2027 or fiscal 2028 in addition to the slots in this RFP.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How will you determine where those slots will be located?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is the timeline to determining providers and beginning those slots?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're in the process of making strategic decisions about how to leverage those slots, specifically to continue to stabilize the ecosystem and strengthening it as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Will there be an impact on providers who are currently providing after-school program services?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So thinking about as you're thinking about those expansions and even thinking about some of the mitigating factors now, how are we looking to leverage some of the losses that we have now, like moving forward?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is there going to be another RFP?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like, what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I would say we are going to move in ways that honor the current procurement process, but also that we are going to be opportunistic about ways to leverage.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The current slots or the future slots in ways that help to mitigate some of the impact.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the appeal timeline. Dana confirms center-based had no extension, school-based received a two-week extension. Escamilla-Davies notes the protest period ends June 4th, with briefings for all non-awardees.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions Compass appeal process for center-based providers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Post-award announcement, there are 10-day appeal process with a submission date of April 24th for the census-based providers that may not have been viable to receive awards.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Has there been any changes in this appeal process?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We extended the appeal process, I believe, by two weeks in order to have providers be able to receive a briefing before they made their appeal.

chapters.summary: Stevens argues closing any community center is unacceptable given rising youth detention numbers and the unique role community-based programs play. She presses for additional OMB funding to fill gaps. Bailey confirms ongoing conversations with OMB.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens challenges closure of community-based Compass centers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I know with the community base, it's a little different than it is for the school base.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And one of my biggest issues with the community base was that we were going to be closing programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And in a time where we are seeing young people having lack of programs, I'm like, well, why would we be closing any programs?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So can we talk about what that process was?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I know...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You guys have been really thoughtful about thinking about creative ways of like trying to work with different providers, but like especially with community.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think in a moment of trying to close any center in this moment sounds crazy to me, especially going into the summer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm just trying to get a better understanding where we are with that and how do we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're in the process of looking at the impact and making strategic decisions about how we move forward because we're in the process of investigating that right now and looking into it deeply.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I don't have a formal strategy to share right now.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Has there been any additional funding for OMB to fill the gaps for these community centers?

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: I think there were an ongoing conversations that the commissioner just mentioned, recognizing the impact of the most recent awards.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And we are looking into how we can appropriately respond to the concerns of all stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And so we're in ongoing conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, I'll join those ongoing conversations because again, I just want to just say like, you know,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Community-based, everybody knows community-based programs has a special place in my heart.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And that's because they do a different type of work.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And the same students that show up at a school-based program typically does not show up at a community center.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And those are a lot of times are the kids we need to be reaching, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Community center staff are the ones that are typically out from the community, out on the ground and in there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so for me in this moment, these community-based programs has to be a priority.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And that has been one of the biggest things that I've been pushing for.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I think like as we're thinking about where we need to save, and I understand that savings, we cannot do it on the back of young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And that is one thing that I'm just not okay with.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I've been saying this, my theme today is like, if we're going to have an affordable city, it needs to be affordable for young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And these are the things that we need, especially when we had ACS before you guys, and we have 362 young people in secure detention currently.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And that is abysmal because when I got into office, we were at 150.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so those numbers have.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: tripled at this point and closing community centers just does not sound like that is something we should be doing in this moment.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm going to continue to advocate and push because community-based programs need to be open and we should be expanding that work and not closing any programs.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the appeal process and 12 schools without awards. Dana explains there were either no viable proposals or no submissions for those schools, and DYCD is reviewing options to ensure no service lapse in the fall.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions school-based appeal and 12 unawarded schools

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I have a couple of questions about the appeal process for the school-based providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is the appeal process the same for the school-based providers that they may, if they were not deemed viable, is it still 10 days as well for them as well?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Yes, so anyone that was not selected for an award, whether center-based or school-based, has the right to appeal.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And it's the same process as well?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And was there an extension for them as well?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So for center-based, there wasn't an extension.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: For the school-based, we gave the extension in the volume.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And a question even around the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Has every contract been awarded already?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So for school base, the majority of the contracts were awarded.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: There were 12 schools right now that currently were not awarded that we're working on getting.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And what does that process look like and why weren't they awarded?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So within that competition, there was either no viable proposal or no one was submitted a proposal for that particular school.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we're right now in conversations to finalize the plan for the 12.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so what does that plan look like and how is that going to work?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is it going to go back out to procurement?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Are we looking at some of the providers that are already in the pool?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm just trying to get an understanding of like how do we then

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like, what does the mitigation for that look like?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: It's still under review for consideration because our ultimate priority is to ensure that services are happening for the fall.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We want to make sure that there's no lapse.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we are looking to see what's the best interest of the city to get services started in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, because I just want to go on record for saying there was a lot of providers who got multiple contracts.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so if they are the people getting additional contracts, I'm going to be upset because I think capacity is one of the things that, you know, we know that a lot of these folks will say they have the capacity.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then when it's time to produce, it starts to get watered down.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I want us to really look at some of these groups who might not have been able to get awarded because there was a lot of competition in certain areas and things like that to kind of mitigate that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But thinking about capacity and being able to, trying to keep some of these providers whole should, and I would like to see that be a priority.

chapters.summary: Stevens raises concerns about new providers obtaining school-age child care licenses by September, noting past SONIC expansion problems. Daniel reports about 200 programs need new licenses, with coordinated inspections underway with DOHMH and NYC Public Schools.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions SAC licensing for new Compass providers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How's the agency assessing providers who do not have school age child care licenses?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because that's another question, especially for the new providers that's coming in that's going to be in these places.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: One of the things I was showing out about when the RFP came out was just like, are we giving additional supports to DOHMH?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because they are a huge part in this process that we kind of forget about.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I'm trying to understand, especially with these new programs coming in, how do we expect to get all these people cleared?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because even if you were cleared at another site, you got to do the process over.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And with the SAC requirements, I'm really concerned about folks being able to start and being able to get up and going by, what is it, September?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: August?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is it?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: September.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: September.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: That's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: At the start of the RFP planning process, we've been in full engagement with the OHMH and New York City Public Schools because there's a whole coordinated process that happens behind the scenes outside of the application process, which includes inspections, the facilities itself.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So we wanted to really focus on the more trouble spots areas and then on the application side, work with those new and existing CBOs to ensure that they are fully understanding the process itself, that we have support and technical assistance that we can provide on our end as well.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: What's been great is that there's only a small subset of these programs starting the fall that actually require a new license itself.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So how many right now we're looking behind the scenes outside of the application process, which includes inspections, the facilities itself.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Looking at just over 200.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So that's a share.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Again, it's awful.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So we wanted to really focus on the more trouble spots areas and then on the application side, work with those new and existing CBOs to ensure that they are fully understanding the process itself, that we have support and technical assistance that we can provide on our end as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You said 200 like it was like 10.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: But let's be clear.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: 200 out of the total 900 plus programs we have.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: We've done this in the past when we did the Sonic expansion.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I was there, and it was not smooth.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: What's been great is that there's only a small subset of these programs starting the fall that actually require a new license itself.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It was not smooth.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Smoother.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I was there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It was not smooth, and it was a mess.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And you're 100% correct on that.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
