utterances.text: And what's been great since then is that there actually is an online portal, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So how many right now we're looking?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: We had to go from paper-based to online since then.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So there's been a lot of progress since then.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And there's a lot of hiccups in there, too.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You know I know the process.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And that is the process of what we do here at DYCD to make sure that we can make those troublesome spots as comfortable as possible, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: I think this is an opportunity for us to really think about these processes across multiple agencies and how we're tracking them and ensuring that we are escalating.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: As we speak right now, we have these teams coordinating and meeting to discuss where additional supports are going to be needed, really coordinating with public schools and identifying the classrooms so the OHMH can go out and do the inspections in a quick manner and engaging with CBOs that still need support in that process.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is there a contingency plan if they are not able to get their SAC license and up and running?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Yes, there will be plans in place.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So you have that plan already?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, so you'll share that with me?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: All right, great.

chapters.summary: Stevens requests lists of non-awarded school-based and center-based providers, non-viable providers, and funded groups with amounts. DYCD agrees to provide the data.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens requests Compass data lists from DYCD

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Can the agency provide the city council with a list of current school-based providers who did not get awards, a new RFP, a list of school-based programs who did not get awarded?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: the new RFP with the old funded amount lines, a list of non-viable center-based providers, and a list of DYCD funded groups for both center-based and school-based awards and their amounts.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You don't have to go through it if you guys can just get that to us.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, we can.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I have a lot of questions, but I'm going to pass it off.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I didn't even get to any of the other sections, so I'll be back.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: She will be back for round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we're going to move to council member questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Council member Maloney, followed by deputy speaker.

chapters.summary: Maloney questions why Manhattan Youth, with 94% attendance and positive audits, was discontinued at some schools but not others. Dana explains each competition is evaluated independently. Escamilla-Davies notes over 80% of principals received their first or second choice.
chapters.title: Councilmember Maloney questions Manhattan Youth Compass award decisions

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: On behalf of countless parents and principals across my district, and you're going to be hearing from other districts in Manhattan, I have concerns about the new Compass RFP,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and in particular Manhattan Youth, which was discontinued at some schools, but not all schools, with limited transparency about why and limited engagement with the parents and the principals on the ground, to Councilmember

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Stevens'point earlier.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: This is a program that had a 94% attendance rate, positive audits, and was recommended first by many principals.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So the question is, why were they deemed insufficient?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And if their program truly was insufficient, then why were they awarded for some contracts and not others, including a case where there's a co-located school where Manhattan Youth was awarded for one school and not.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: The other.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I want to say that, you know, I want to speak more broadly about the selection process, and I'm going to hand it over to Dana.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I do want to talk about this in terms of transparency and the narratives that are out there.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This was a procurement process that was highly transparent.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The concept paper was co-developed by the field.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And in any procurement, there are winners and non-winners.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And my heart goes out to all of the agencies that were not awarded.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I am really proud of the rigorous process that we engaged in and the level of care that DYCD took in making these awards.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I want to speak about that, but I also want to give you the technical answer, which I'm going to hand over to Dana.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I also want to say that every single competition in every single region was competing with other providers.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so they were ranked in that geographical area accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So there were some providers that in some areas they were rewarded, awarded their specific, they were re-awarded, and in other areas they were not.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I can tell you on the provider side, that is, as someone who's been a provider, that is devastating, and yet it is part of the procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I think the thing I want to highlight, because this has not been procured for over 10 years, there is a sense of ownership that I think is really felt in terms of relationships.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: in terms of continuity.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And yet what we know to be true is that procurement is necessary because it allows for innovation, for equity, and for accountability.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So I think more than one thing could be true about this process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But again, I share the deep empathy and regard for the staff, for the families and the children, youth that are going to have a new provider.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I'm also really excited about the providers that were selected that are high quality, high caliber, and that offer really great services.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: To add, I definitely want to push back a little on the fact that the procurement wasn't transparent.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: It was extremely transparent.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We had made selections based on a basis for award where there was criteria outlined on how we were making the awards.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: In the case of Manhattan Youth, they did apply to multiple competitions, and each competition is handled on its own merit.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So you could have one school, which was considered a competition, be more competitive than others, and we made selections based on where scores, ranking, provided diversity.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: There were many areas that we looked at when making those selections, but it stood behind the basis for award that was released in the RFP, and that was a procurement document that was made public.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: You shared what the technical factors are that you were looking at.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: One of them was around budget.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Can you share more details on how Manhattan Youth scored against those criteria?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: At this time, we can't give specifics because we are in the protest period.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And also, they will be awarded a briefing, so they will get not only their scores and how they fared in relationship to other people, but they will get also specific feedback on their RFP of areas that were highlighted by the evaluator.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And are there instances where a school and a principal did rank Manhattan Youth as excellent and it was still not awarded again?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And if that's the case, there's a process where the principals on the ground are being overruled in a closed-door process, if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It wasn't a closed-door process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It was actually a transparent process that was guided by a contribution that was made by the principal.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So principals'selections was weighed, but the primary selections, as Dana mentioned, were the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so I'm proud to say that over 80% of principals got their first or second award.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And with principals that did not get their first ranking, we're going to be working to help that transition on the ground to really think about ways to be a bridge and to make sure that they are centering this choice around children, youth, and families.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And my last question, I know I'm out of time here, is if there's a chance to re-evaluate these decisions, and we're signing up for a six-year RFP, so if these

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: programs are not meeting the needs of the community.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: For example, in this case, there are scopes of services like sports, arts, or academic enrichment that the prior provider did offer that the new provider does not, which can be very disruptive for the youth and the families.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And that's who I'm representing today.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: What's the recourse on the line?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I want to say two things.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The first is that I know how resilient young people are and how adaptable they are to change.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I also know how responsive providers are to children, youth, and families and how they will tailor their services to meet the needs of young people.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yes, it is a new process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yes, there will be an on-ramp.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I believe that at the end of this, that there will be responsive services that center young people.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And yet, if they do not, like any other process at DYCD, we will take the necessary course of action.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Just really quickly, I know you guys said that you're going to be doing a briefing with the providers and a debrief around the folks who aren't viable.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Can you please make sure you share the information with us and also let us know when that's happening so we can possibly participate and support?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Deputy Speaker, followed by Councilmember Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Oh, wait.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, Deputy Speaker first, followed by you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks about budgetary actions for racial equity goals and the equitable investment methodology timeline. Chief Equity Officer Ramirez confirms goals are integrated into operations, with poverty as the primary driver (70%+), and full implementation by 2034 aligned with RFP cycles.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Hudson questions DYCD racial equity plan and equitable investment methodology

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm going to ask some questions about the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Recently, the administration released a preliminary report on the plan.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: The plan included input from many city agencies, including your own.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Could you just share with us if any of the goals that you have identified have any budgetary actions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: I'm Denise Ramirez, and I serve as the Chief Equity and Inclusion Officer here at DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Yes, there are budget implications for our

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: goals and our strategies, but the way we've approached this is to incorporate it into our operations.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: We have a goal on capacity building that's part of the budget for this year.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: We have the equity methodology, which will be integrated into

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: the RFPs as we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So it is integrated into how we approach the work.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm happy you mentioned the methodology.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So in 2021, you began developing an equitable investment methodology that prioritizes communities with the highest concentrations of poverty.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: However, the city's racial

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Equity Plan states that this methodology will not be fully applied to all city tax levy funded programs until 2034.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Why will it take more than a decade to fully implement this approach and what barriers are preventing faster implementation?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: The timeline of 2034 is to align with what we expect to be the cycle of RFPs coming online.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: I will say I think the hurdles are what we've been talking about is anytime that we are implementing change, it will take time to iterate, adapt, and integrate the methodology.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So it's part of the process.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I just think that's very long to do, especially considering you guys started this in 2021.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So one of your long-term goals is to apply this investment methodology to 100% of the funded programs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: What percentage of the city tax levy funded programs currently use this methodology?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: It was applied to the literacy RFP in 2024, and an equity approach was applied to the Compass RFP.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So those are the two so far.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Can you explain how this methodology weighs poverty data, demographic data, community violence indicators, and existing service gaps when allocating?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: program slots?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: The primary driver for the methodology is poverty, looking at poverty data.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So you don't use any other metrics of data, only poverty?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: It's the driver.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So poverty makes up at least 70% in

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: In some cases, like the Literacy RFP, we took into account the program-specific needs.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So looking at data on limited English proficiency or looking at data on adults without a high school diploma.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So we will look at other factors, but poverty is the primary driver.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: But when do you look at other factors?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Yes, yes.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: No, but I'm saying when.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: When do you say, let's look at other factors?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: As part of the process.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So we'll look at, in the Compass RFP, we looked at students with disabilities, students in temporary housing.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: We looked at English language learners.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So as part of that process, we also looked at the priority precincts with the highest levels of gun violence.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So as part of that process, we will look at that.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, so you do look at other data metrics, but primarily property.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, and just the last question, will DYCD publicly release the methodology and underlying data used to determine which neighborhoods receive priority investments?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So one of the strategies that we included

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: in this long-term goal is to conduct outreach and engagement to get input on the methodology.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: So as part of that process, we will be sharing, as we do that outreach, sharing what's been done and soliciting feedback.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein, followed by Councilmember Joseph.

chapters.summary: Epstein raises Henry Street's 150-year history and asks how families will be served. He challenges the lack of parent input in the RFP process, calling it a mistake. Escamilla-Davies commits to visiting schools and speaking with parents before the school year ends.
chapters.title: Councilmember Epstein questions center-based provider cuts and parent input

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I just want to first focus on center-based providers that lost their funding.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know we've talked a lot about this, especially, you know, Henry Street in my district.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's got a 150-year history providing services, has a Boys and Girls Republic Center that now, you know, how are we going to make them whole to ensure families who have been relying on that place for decades are going to be able to have services for their children there?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're in the process of evaluating a strategy to really think about strengthening stability and continuity.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so we're in the thick of that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So what's our timeline to get for them to hear back from you?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I believe that the protesting period ends June 4th.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yeah, June 4th, and everybody gets a briefing.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So thereafter, we hope to be able to identify kind of some next steps.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, because they have staff, and so they have staffing needs, and they're making some decisions.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We need to be able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then families also have to make decisions based on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So you think soon after June 5th you'll be able to get back to them?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And just on the other providers, the school-based providers, I want to follow up with Councilmember Maloney in some of these determinations.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How much did family input play into this factor?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How much did principal input play in?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How much did services, like someone has a sports program, someone doesn't have a sports program, like all those factors cost?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How are these all factored in?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Because, you know, from the families we're hearing and hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of families, you know, they were very happy with the provider.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I understand that you went through a process and I respect that, but it feels like they're not feeling like their input has been heard.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Dana, would you like to?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So as part of the procurement process, the principals did have an opportunity to weigh in through their ranking.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: And when we made those selections, we did take other factors into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: The parents at the time of the RFP, that was not taken into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: However, that will be done as part of the school partnership agreement process when we're bringing the new providers in.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: That's when the opportunity for the parents to speak about what programming they want to ensure that the new provider is providing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I appreciate that, but I'm not sure why after the fact you would take parents'consideration in.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Like, why not have that involved in the application process and parent engagement?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And are they happy?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I appreciate the principals and the three schools that lost Manhattan Youth in my district already.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Ranked them as number one.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So they've already said they want them to continue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The parents said they want them to continue, but not really listening to the parents.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The second question I'll ask, because I know I'm almost out of time, is you committed to coming out to the school to talk to parents, or your team will be?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We'd like to be able to get that set up before the end of the school year.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to make a commitment now to make sure that happens in the next couple weeks.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I'm committed to speak to whoever wants to speak to me about this process.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, just on the...

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Yeah, I also just want to add, in terms of parent engagement, we also, prior to the RFP being released as part of our stakeholder engagement, we did engage families to build that new compass model that made it into the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So parents were engaged before, but during the actual RFP process, it was really between the school principals and DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I'll just say I think that's a mistake, honestly.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I think parents are the recipients of these services, and they're the ones to best judge beyond the paper what happens day to day in those schools.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I think taking their consideration out of the mix as an instrumental part of what we're doing, I think, unfortunately, was a mistake.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I think it'll have some consequences in our district, and we're hearing directly from it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I would love to figure that out.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I appreciate your willingness to come talk to parents and to hear directly from them, but also hearing directly from the community about the jobs and the staffing and making sure people who are working for Manhattan Youth, maybe they can, you know.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: either stay on with a new provider or whatever we can do to fix this problem, we really do need to fix it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just wanted to add something to that, to the principal engagement and to how extensively through this process the superintendents were involved and the principals were involved.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But where we are right now, having just announced these awards and finalizing the protest period, is precisely the time when families and young people and principals and superintendents can express

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: what it is that they love about their current provider, and how those programs and services could be implemented in the fall, what they haven't had that they'd like to see.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We love at DYCD, you won't get any arguments, when a school community loves their after school provider, that is like the best thing for us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And we really appreciate the advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: If that energy could be brought to

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: to help plan and coordinate with the new provider.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is what our kids want.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We have a whole group who wants theater.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We can't live without soccer here.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you hire with that in mind?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Like this is the time for the school specific, student specific activities to be negotiated.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And that's what Daniel and the team are gonna be engaging with providers in now.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I hope that the adults working with young people around this will take that opportunity to engage with the new providers to get things off to a good start in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know my time is up, Chair, so I'll come back for a second round.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I will add you to the...

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about the Summer Rising budget ($151M for FY27) and reports of FY25 payment delays. Bailey reports 98% of FY27 amendments are registered and less than 1% of invoices are over 30 days, encouraging providers to contact the help desk for issues.
chapters.title: Councilmember Joseph questions Summer Rising budget and provider payments

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph, followed by Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, and I hope young people are also at the table when you're making these decisions and their voices are centered.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You know what my favorite topic is?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What is the total budget for Summer Rising program?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And can you provide the status update on number of providers for the Summer Rising program remaining outstanding or not receiving payments for FY25 service?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You know the city love to owe people.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How we doing on that?

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Well, hello.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: So summarizing, our budget for FR27 is $151 million.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: We're grateful that the most recent administration did it based on restoration and the JAN plan.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And so we know for this upcoming

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Being summer, the funding is stored and will have continuity of services.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: What was your other question?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We're hearing from providers that haven't been paid from FY25.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: All of our FY26 amendments, inclusive of summarizing, are pretty much registered.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: I'll just get the exact number.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: But for the most part, for FY27,

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: 98% of our amendments associated with all types of increases to funding have been moved.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And this is FY25.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: They wanted to know why is there delays and providers have been informed of why there's a delay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Do you inform the providers when there's a delay in payments?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What do you say to them?

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: For payments, usually we inform them that there's a delay, but currently there has not been any substantial delay in payments.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: I think what they're referencing is some may be saying they have not received their funding, additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: If that's the case, I ask that they reach out to our help desk and we can look into it.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: But to my knowledge, all implementations for prior summers, for summer rising, weren't included in a release to providers.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We can always talk offline and let you know who the provider is.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What strategies are being implemented for pay providers faster in the FY26?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What is the timeline to get funding out the door right away for providers?

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Well, currently, most of our providers, when we get contracts registered, we do a 50% advance to help with cash flow.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: After that, we essentially do reimbursements based on provider invoice submission.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Now, in full transparency, we have not had as much invoice submissions, and so that may be impacting the provider.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Carter's ability to maintain cash flow.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: But currently we have less than 1% of invoices under 30 days, I mean over 30 days.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: So we know that there's a pretty much fast turnaround for invoice submissions.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: But again, if someone is having specific issues, I encourage you, please reach out and we'll look into it.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: But right now, about 1% of our invoices are over 30 days.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And I think when I came in and looked, it was about three, and they're at 40, and I said work on them.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: So I know that they're already working on them right now.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And so we're prioritizing.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Wonderful to hear.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And if a provider's having trouble accessing the system, do you provide technical support for that provider?

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Yes, we have a help desk, and a lot of our staff are doing phone calls all the time, and we work closely with MOX.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: Our payment team and budget team have weekly calls with MOX to troubleshoot any issues they're having technically.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And if providers are having issues, we always raise those concerns to them during those meetings.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: And again, we kind of provide our system.

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: We have a help desk to respond to any increase.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many students applied for summer rising today, and what provision are you putting in place for students with special needs, students with IEPs?

utterances.speaker: Navita Bailey
utterances.text: I'm going to defer to the youth services team to respond to that.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So this past year we received over 150,000 applications.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So really excited about that.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: In regarding providing supports for youth and disabilities, we've been working with the DIAL team in New York City Public Schools to ensure that we have accommodation plans for every single

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: child that is required to receive those services and coordinating with school leadership and the CBOs as well to ensure that all those supports in place for all those services throughout the program period.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And a student who needs a one-to-one para in summarizing, one will be provided and you will accommodate what's on the IEP.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Correct?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Restler presses on how one provider can score excellently at one location but below threshold at another, citing a Hasidic community provider. Dana explains different evaluator teams and varying competition levels. Escamilla-Davies adds nuance about competitive density.
chapters.title: Councilmember Restler questions Compass scoring inconsistency across sites

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you so much to the esteemed and impressive co-chairs of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Really appreciate your great leadership.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And congratulations again, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I really enjoyed sitting down with you some weeks ago and checking in on some of the compass concerns that we've been hearing from our communities.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And, you know, I just think it's a great thing to have somebody with such deep expertise in youth development leading the agency.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And we know there's a really strong bench supporting you at DYCD and look forward to working with you and your team.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I continue to be concerned about kind of the compass process.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I fully appreciate that, you know, whenever we put out a new RFP, I mean, part of the reason we haven't put out this RFP for 10 years is because they're winners and losers and it's an incredibly bumpy process to go through.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So the last administration handed you this, you know, hot potato, I guess I'll call it, for the purposes of being diplomatic.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But, you know, as I've shared with you, we have, just to give one example, a provider that serves the Hasidic community in Williamsburg that's been an excellent provider for many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They had hundreds of seats.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They now have dozens.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And they were the one provider that served that community.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And they scored really well.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know you're not going to speak to an individual provider, but I'm going to just give you the specific context for my district to make this real.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so I think we want to, it would help me to understand, like, how could one provider score excellently at one location and above the threshold, but not at a different location when it's the same team providing the same excellent services across the board?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So within each competition, there's evaluators that are reading, and the evaluating team could be different across the board, right?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So it's all objective based on how the provider has demonstrated that they can provide these services.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: And the other part, too, is that depending on how...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You're saying objective, but isn't that inherently subjective, right?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Like that is the precise definition of a subjective process is that it's different readers, and some readers are determining that this organization is excellent, and two blocks away where they have a different site, they're determining they're not.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: That is, I mean, sorry to interrupt you, but.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: No, no, no, it's fine.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: And, but I would also just add that it really depends on what other providers competed in that competition, right?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: If the.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: If the program was, sorry, if the competition was extremely competitive, that's where you're going to see the disparity in sports.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I can appreciate that you could have different, at a given location, some other organization applied and is even more excellent than the group that's been there for 30 years, and they get selected because they did even better.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I struggle to understand is how one organization can be reviewed as excellent at this location, but at these other locations in the neighborhood where they've been doing an excellent job for 30 years and always scored well on all of their DYCD contracts and all of their performance reviews,

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: how they could be found to have such a different score based on who's reading it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It just, that is what really pains me and is paining my community.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I want to, I appreciate what you're saying, and I do think this is very nuanced.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And what I would say is, I just, I think there's assumption in some of the questions, right?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The assumption is that they did poorly in one section and good in another section.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And the truth is that they could have done really well, but there was greater competition in one, and there were more providers that were invited or that came to that proposal.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So if you have 10 in one section and you have five in another and you rank number seven and there are six slots, so I want to just offer that nuance, but I don't want to take away from what you spoke about earlier.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So it's not like that they did poorly, right, if we give that example, and then better in another one.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It's really that they might have done really well in both, but there was greater competition.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, from our conversation, previous conversation, Commissioner, you had explained that there was a threshold level that if organizations did above, then you could try to figure out a path to work with them because they scored well enough on the thing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And if they scored below that threshold level, then your hands are kind of tied because they didn't, you know, when you have an objective, subjective, whatever word we want to use, you have a process, it was determined.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And that's what I'm speaking to in my question.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: is that on some locations they didn't score above that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm just using this as an example, but there are other providers, and I was on a tour this morning of one site with a city agency assistant commissioner who wanted me to ask questions about their school.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So every Manhattan youth, if Virginia's still here, but everybody's concerned about, I think, a similar thing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I just am using this as an example.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This organization scores above the threshold, you know, here, doesn't across the street, it's confusing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so I, can I ask one more question?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Are you going to kill me?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I can't do round two, but it's okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I will text you the question.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's about charter schools getting selected for the Compass RFP, and that is what I'm concerned about.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But I will save that for out the year.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Chair Stevens will ask it in a much more effective way.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Selvena Brooks-Powers, followed by Councilmember Brewer.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks how $14.8M in CMS expansion will reduce gun violence and about year-round employment. Daryl details place-based CMS work and the ONS transition to the Office of Community Safety. Ramirez highlights Work Learn Grow serving 7,000 youth with college credit opportunities.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brooks-Powers questions CMS funding and year-round youth employment

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, and hello, Commissioner, and to the DYCD team.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The increase of $14.8 million of city funding in fiscal 27 to expand services for the crisis management system program is welcome news.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: My community has seen a surge in gun violence in the last few months, including in a neighboring district where the deputy speaker represents with the tragic shooting of 15-year-old Jaden Pierre in Southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How will this new funding allocation help reduce gun violence, particularly among our youth?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What CMS groups will see additional funding thanks to this increase?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: and how will the transition of CMS from DYCD to the Office of Community Safety Impact Programming and the city's administration of these programs, because we know when it shifted from Mach J to DYCD, it was a whole

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: dynamic there.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then also for my CMS providers, they're often going above and beyond, going beyond the small territory they're responsible for, and the funding that they have right now is absolutely not sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And the last question, I'll leave you with, and I can repeat whatever you need me to.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: SYEP meets a critical gap for youth to seek gainful employment and stay busy during the summer months.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But we have heard that youth need similar employment opportunities throughout the academic school year.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What is DYCD doing to meet this need?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm going to start.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Thank you for that question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I'm going to start with the transition of ONS from DYCD to the Office of Community Safety, and then I'll pass it over to my colleague, Daryl.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The first thing I want to say is that I am really excited about the Deputy Mayor, Renita Francois, and her commitment to

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: really seeing this program through and to making it a priority for the whole city.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so the transition is going to take place in two phases.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The first is the programmatic and strategic transition has already occurred.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So it's a transition.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It is already reporting to the new commissioner and to the deputy mayor.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And that has been seamless, and it has been happening in tandem with the support of my team.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The operational aspects of it, the contracts and some of the operations, are going to be transitioned over the last year, over the next year.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: What I can say is that because we had the experience of transitioning from MACJ to DYCD,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: we have experience in what this could look like in ways that really keep programming intact and then do not disrupt services.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so we're all committed to doing that and to continue to partner with the deputy mayor as well as the new commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And then, Daryl, you can speak specifically to the other question.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I dare you.

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: Yes, and I would need to be sweating.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member, and it's great to see you.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: You too, Darrell.

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: So we are working closely with the Office of Community Safety on the additional investment of $14.8 million for the Office of Neighborhood Safety.

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: We're working out details now on both how that's going to be.

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: Either amended or procured and which groups that it's going to, but it will support the place-based work, the precision work that that team is doing on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: So supporting the crisis management system, supporting the wraparound services, supporting the

utterances.speaker: Daryl
utterances.text: The HFIP hospital program as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I just want to put a plug in for Hamels and expansion to Hamels because right now I'm using discretionary funding, which is fine, but they have not been designated an official site.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so we have King of Kings that is stretching pretty much most of the peninsula.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then the supplemental funding I give to Queens Royal Priesthood is like a stopgap.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So we need to expand those as especially as the summertime comes.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then the question on SYEP.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: I'm really excited about it because we agree.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: We have our Work, Learn, and Grow program, which was created due to advocacy from the council many years ago.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: That program serves 7,000 young people who were in SYEP and are able to extend their experiences through the school year.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And we've found that the impact of that program really

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: is exponential on top of what they get through SYEP because we're able to engage them throughout the school year.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: We're able to offer college credits.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: A lot of the young people who go through Work, Learn, and Grow have received a college credit, over 20,000 of them in the past five years.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And we have another 1,000 people with our Learn and Earn program, which is also a full-year program.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And so I would say we definitely appreciate your advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: There's tremendous, tremendous interest from young people for Work, Learn, and Grow slots.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And so it's definitely a place that as an agency we're looking to continue investing in and looking at how we can do more.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that, and I hope it is expanded.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: My very first on-the-books job was summer youth employment program, and then I also worked through the school year.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It wasn't back then called what it's called now, but it definitely was helpful to get money in my own pocket.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So I advocate for that, and I know our amazing chair, Stevens, does as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about subcontracting (30% allowed), reports principals lack contact info for new providers, and asks how contracting improvements are measured. Dana describes weekly meetings with MOX, internal tracking systems, and the backlog initiative implemented three years ago.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions Compass subcontracting and contracting metrics

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Wong.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate the call yesterday that we discussed the...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: after school programs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I sent you a letter.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I assume you got it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And we hope that that can be changed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it true that we could do sub contracting if nothing else works?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: The contract does allow for 30% subcontracting.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, that's not everything then, okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Also, just so you know, the people, principals do not have a contact for the other provider.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't know how to reach them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I obviously know, so I made the contact, but they did not have a contact, just so you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They contacted you to get a contact.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I hope that can be changed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's really causing a lot of challenges.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Also, PS145 is not happy.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They want LEAP.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I assume you know that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They also contacted me.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Everybody contacts me.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so they, too, are upset.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we've got a lot of upset people, just FYI.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In terms of contracting, we work, we have wonderful staff, we work constantly, really almost with a lot of agencies in Manhattan, not just in my district.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the issue is constantly, I wonder what is the benchmarks or what metrics do you use to evaluate whether the contracting process has improved and what do those metrics show compared to previous years?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And also, how many do you keep track of how many get escalated to the mayor's office of non-profit services, which is what we do.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We call them and then work with them to try to get contracting addressed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something, do you keep track of that?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we do work very closely with the Mayor's Office of Contract Services.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We meet with them weekly.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We have our own tracking monitoring system where we are able to track where the contract is within the life cycle that's outside of Passport.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we do have, we are able to pull data from our own internal systems as well as from Passport on where things fare in the contracting process.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So how do you measure that it's working better?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we have also weekly meetings.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We have contracting weeklies.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We have a program operations team that meets with us to ensure that we're meeting our regular goals and targets.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: This was all implemented as part of the backlog initiative that was put in place two years ago, I think, three years.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we are actively monitoring and also working on improving our lifetime cycle for registration.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and then I guess as a three-year, I remember when the three-year contract process went into effect, we are hearing that people don't find that it's more, I thought it would really be much better, but they don't feel that it is as

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: productive, even more so than it was in the past.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you know how many providers chose not to apply or express confusion about the new model?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: I don't have those numbers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you get them to us?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Yeah, we'll have to also work with the Mayor's Office of Contract Services because although there are three-year contract term, it still has to go through the clear list process as well as the annual application that providers have to file with council.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because they feel like there's not enough communication so that they understand the process, just so you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm getting these because we get everything, people contacting us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Wilson.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about no-show children and waitlisted families not receiving calls. Daniel explains the collaborative model with schools and CBOs, notes thousands more offers accepted than last year, and confirms both schools and CBOs have waitlist access to fill vacant seats.
chapters.title: Councilmember Wong questions Summer Rising attendance and waitlist management

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: My question, my first question is regards to the attendance for the summer rising.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Okay, so we have over 150,000 applicants were submitted from March 3rd to March 27th.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: My experience with summarizing is that quite often some kids don't show up, and then meanwhile there are other kids in the waiting list.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: They're expecting a phone call, and they never get the call.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: So my question is who does the outreach, the YCD, or is the school's job?

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Because when I call the school, I get the runaround.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: You know, I think it's important to note that this is a collaborative program model between New York City Public Schools and DYCD and ensuring that there are supports and services for all young people.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And also summer is a very different model in itself, right?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: It is not a requirement.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: to show up every single day.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: We have families that utilize space and weeks and days differently than you would say after school, which is a year-round component.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Another thing is that the wait list is also factored in with other components, such as ensuring that we are prioritizing youth that are in special needs,

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: students in temporary housing, you know, families that will be able to have opportunity to have those seats that they probably would have never used before.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And it is an extremely popular model, so we are going to see robust wait lists in certain areas.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Then, you know, from one year to the next.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So every year we do focus on looking at specific locations and sites where we can open up more programs.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And actually this year we actually opened up more programs to kind of lighten the load on wait lists for specific schools.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: This past year we opened up 40 new elementary programs where we know the demand has increased.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: But are families in the waiting list, are they being contacted and say, okay, we have an opening, please come?

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Because my impression is that they don't get these phone calls.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: I'm glad you asked that question.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And as my colleague was mentioning, like every year we listen to feedback and we make improvements.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: We had roughly the same amount of applications this year as last year, but we made significantly more offers, thousands more offers.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: At this time, in comparison to the same time last year, we also have thousands more of those offers accepted.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So we're above, by the thousands, the numbers that we had last year.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: And the wait list is actually...

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: gotten smaller because of those efforts.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So as Commissioner said in her testimony, looking at the attendance patterns, as you mentioned, enrollment patterns, we've been able to get more young people pushed into the program than we ever have before,

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: being able to anticipate some of those trends, and I think we'll see changes to the experience you had last summer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Oh, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Back to my question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: If kids don't show up, say, after the first week, should the next person move up?

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Is that happening?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: It's happening.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: And does DYCD reaches out or the school reaches out?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So both the school and CBO have access to the same wait list.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So either school or CBO will be doing some engagement as the list moves.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: So if they're not receiving a call, unfortunately, that may mean that the list hasn't moved up to the next person.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: There's also that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Quick question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Quick.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: City or state, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: The $246 million for SYEP, is that primarily city funds or state?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: It's primarily city funding with a small allocation of TANF funding from the state.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: As I recall, SYEP historically gets state funding, or am I wrong here?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: The scale of the state funding is very small in comparison to the amount of city tax levy that we have funding the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: I see it's so successful.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: I think the size of SYEP should increase, and I don't see that happening.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: So please look into that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Won
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Wilson, followed by Banks.

chapters.summary: Wilson adds to Manhattan Youth concerns with 400+ parent complaints and a CEC resolution. He also raises Hudson Guild's cut leaving a gap for NYCHA families in Chelsea. Escamilla-Davies acknowledges the impact and says strategies are being evaluated, with updates expected after June 6th.
chapters.title: Councilmember Wilson questions Manhattan Youth and Hudson Guild cuts

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would also like to add my voice with that of my colleagues Maloney and Epstein regarding Manhattan Youth.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I've received an overwhelming correspondence to my office, over 400 parents and students and teachers concerned about the change.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, they received positive audits and built decades-long relationships with the school communities and parent communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They've been told that the new programs are going to look to...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Incorporate the same staff or programs that existed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So my question is, if we're looking to keep what was built by Manhattan Youth, why switch providers?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Because procurement processes allow for competition, which is necessary for the ecosystem of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so while, again, I wanted to say that I appreciate the advocacy, and my heart goes out.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: to the children, youth, and families of those communities.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I also want to say that I have confidence that the new provider will be able to meet the needs of the communities as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The CEC2 in my district also put out a resolution asking for them to be reinstated.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, given that response from the parents and the school board, would there be any consideration to reinstating Manhattan Youth?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So what I would say is that the school procurement process was different than the center-based process because the school procurement process allowed for continuity of the slots, the number of seats in a building.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so what is being asked of us is essentially would we give the contract to another provider that won it through the competition?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Unless we found something egregious in the RFP process, and believe me, I am reading a lot of these close calls to make sure, but it's really the continuity of the slots and not the provider.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I also want to ask about the CBO selection.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I want to acknowledge that Hudson Guild is here with us today, which is a crucial provider in Council District 3, centered in Chelsea, right next to two very important NYCHA developments and families that they serve.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they were a recipient of one of the cuts, and this leaves a huge gap.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In the district for after school programming with the provider on the northern edge.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's not really going to work for parents and for the families, especially those in NYCHA that really rely on this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how are we going to deal with that gap?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how is Hudson Guild going to make sure that they have the continued resources they need to serve Manhattan and my council district?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yeah, I would say that we're in the process of thinking about continuity of services and stability of the ecosystem for center-based awards.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And we are hopeful, as I mentioned earlier, to...

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: To share where we land after June 6th.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The DYCD funds programs explicitly designed to improve educational outcomes for high-need students.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What are the challenges DYCD faces in understanding whether those programs are reaching the students who need them the most and whether they're working?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how can the New Visions portal potentially help address those changes?

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: I want to understand more which programs you're talking about at DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Are you talking about, I mean, on the one hand, all of our programs are designed to help young people to reach their goals and to thrive.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: I'm not sure which particular program you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So let me, I have to.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I can answer a portion of that.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I think, Valerie, if you can chime in as well.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I think one of the things that New Visions offers the portal is access to data that community-based organizations would ordinarily not have to assess impact.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Right now, currently, all of our contracts are designed to check enrollment and participation.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But with the support of New Visions for Public Schools portals, we're able to look at how that correlates to academic gains.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Yeah, so I oversee our workforce program, so primarily young people between the ages of 14 and 24.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And we have been in really amazing conversations with the New Visions team about why we can leverage their tool, because we are always looking at ways to demonstrate the specific impacts, especially of the Summer Youth Employment Program and Work, Learn, Grow, which I talked about

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: a little earlier.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And so we've been looking at things, we already do this, but we've been looking at expanding for a school.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Can they see a young person's participation in summer youth employment programs so that they can take that into consideration as they're developing what a high school experience is going to look like for that young person?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And then in return, can we look at how that young person did throughout the program now that we have that data in that portal and can see comprehensively how their experience was in a public school?

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And so I'm really proud over the last many years we've been building that partnership with public schools.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: And we're now in conversation with New Visions about how we can leverage their tool.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I just want to add that that's super critical in terms of allowing providers to use data to inform their practice.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so we are hopeful to continue to be working with them and expand our work with New Visions in the coming years.

utterances.speaker: Daniel
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Denise Ramirez
utterances.text: Councilmember Banks.

chapters.summary: Banks challenges the concentration of 80+ school contracts with one provider and raises a small CBO with a 10-year track record that lost its award despite principal support. Dana says the large provider makes up 10% of awards. Escamilla-Davies notes 52 new providers joined the portfolio.
chapters.title: Councilmember Banks questions contract concentration and small CBO exclusion

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Good to see you again, Commissioner, and to your team.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: One of the, obviously, topics of discussion and contention has been the Compass Program, or RFP.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: One of the major concerns raised...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Following the most recent Compass RFP process involves the concentration of contracts amongst a smaller number of large providers.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: In some instances, a single organization reportedly oversees north of 80 schools or sites under the DYCD-funded Compass program.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
