utterances.text: What is the agency's rationale for allowing one provider to manage such a large portfolio of school-based contracts?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So at time of award selection, we really did take that into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: The provider that you're referencing, they did submit a lot of proposals across the entire procurement and weren't selected for everything that they proposed for, and they do make up only 10% of all the awards that were made.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We did not award them in multiple competitions just to ensure that we were reaching other providers.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: That's a large concentration of contracts to one organization.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: They are an existing provider, and when we were making rewards, we wanted to ensure that we were making them thoughtful and not overgrowing the system.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: And we really did make an effort to ensure that we were reaching other providers within the different competitions.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Well, that particular provider is actually, they received contracts in, I mean, areas that they have no track record in as far as community relations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So how do you rationalize that?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Yeah, within their response for the RFP, they demonstrated to be the highest proposer within those competitions.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I think your point is well taken.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I want to also just say there's 52 new providers as a result of that.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So in prior iterations, we had higher concentrations of what you're speaking of.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And this has really opened up the aperture and created ecosystems that's more balanced.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: That takes into account small, medium, and large organizations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Well, many of the smaller community-based organizations feel that they are being shut out of the opportunities within the Compass ecosystem, despite years of...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: service and strong relationships within their communities, what opportunities currently exist for the smaller nonprofits to grow within the compass, and what opportunity, and

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: broader DYCD, and the broader DYCD ecosystem, and compete for additional sites or contracts over time, including programs such as

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Saturday Night Lights and other DYCD-funded active initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What are being done to support those groups or give them opportunities?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As the Commissioner mentioned, we have 52 new providers as a result of this Compass RFP.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And in terms of small CBOs, 17, which are considered those that have less than $100,000

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: in revenue, we do have a whole host of capacity building services to help those providers along the way as they enter into the Compass portfolio.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In terms of new procurements at this time, we don't have anything for them to apply to, but they do have the opportunities also to serve as subcontractors that help them get into these Compass programs and start building their ability to.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I have one, there's one organization that's been in the school for close to 10 years or so.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: A long, long track record in that community.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And they've been surviving off of CASA monies that we've given them.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And then whatever other monies that they've raised, they competed for a compass, you know, they submitted for the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And they're actually, they got approved, the principal supported them.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: The consideration was given.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But they didn't get the contract.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And that to me means something because having a relationship with the community, having a relationship with the students means a lot.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I know the commissioner had mentioned earlier, I think it was the commissioner, the need to support those new organizations that are coming in and figure out ways that, you know,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: The community can now instruct them or give them ideas of things that they want to see.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But a relationship is important.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And when that chemistry is broken up, it can do damage to the community.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I know you're looking at numbers, you have your scorecards, but those relationships mean a lot.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I don't want our community to be negatively impacted because of a score chart.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: That really doesn't take into the human side of the impact of those relationships.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I know this is ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I know a lot of these organizations have put in appeals, and we're hoping that strong consideration is taken, given to those organizations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And can I just ask one more question, Madam Chair, if you could indulge me?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: This will end it.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I'll pass on those other questions.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you for your indulgence.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When it comes to contract processing and staff reimbursement, this has been a major issue with a lot of the discretionary allocations to a lot of the smaller organizations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What is being done?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: You know, my office, I think every council office acts as a medium between the DYCD and those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But there are many times when DYCD should just directly call those organizations if there are issues that occur or documents are missing.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Sometimes there's like a major lapse between communications between DYC and those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What we want to do is see how we can strengthen that with DYC directly.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: It's being more proactive and reaching out to those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Trust me, my office, we can be dealing with a whole host of other issues instead of having to be this medium or this go-between between NYCHA and the nonprofit.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So if we can improve that, that will go a long way.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I'm sure the commissioner will take that into consideration and add that to the million list of things to do.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about preventing last summer's debit card fraud issues. Ramirez details expanded financial literacy training, provider convenings, and coordination with banks, law department, and payroll processors to prevent recurrence.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens lightning round: SYEP debit card fraud prevention

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm just going to do a quick lightning round around some things because I know we do have to wrap very quickly.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: My first question is just around SYEP.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Hey, Val.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: There you go.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Just thinking about as we prepare into the summer and going to the summer and thinking about the issue we had last summer with the debit cards and things like that, I just want to get a clear understanding of how are we moving forward this summer to ensure that that doesn't take place again and ensuring that, you know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Young people are getting paid in a timely manner and other things are happening?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can assure you that the team at DYCD is doing everything within our control to prevent it from happening again.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I mean, I can talk about this for a very long time because it's been our focus since the day it happened last year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But high level, we're doing everything from engaging young people in more and more financial literacy training.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our providers have been convened at DYCD to make sure they're focused on educating their young people on the risks of participating in fraud.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we're working with the banks, the law department, DUI, the payroll processor, and all of those folks to understand what they're doing to prevent it from happening too.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know we have been encompassed crazy, but I will call you so we can have a deeper briefing to go over how do we prevent and what does that look like moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Really quickly, this might be Bob, but over here, so get ready.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This is adult literacy.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the $20M Education for Integration and Equity proposal (no pointed OMB conversation yet) and the Fatherhood Initiative budget ($8.2M with 14 providers across 17 contracts in all five boroughs).
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions adult literacy $20M proposal and Fatherhood Initiative budget

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Just have some questions around there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know who works for what.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The New York City Coalition of Adult Literacy has responded to the threats with a $20 million proposal for the mayoral administrators to create a new fund stream for immigrant adult literacy students called Education for Integration Equality.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Are those conversations with OMB to add this new funding stream into the adopted budget?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have you guys started having those conversations?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, we didn't swear him in.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Bob, for being honest.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I do, sir.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm sorry, could you restate the last part of the question?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I was asking, you know, the adult coalition has been asking about this $20 million proposed for additional funding for immigrant adult literacy students.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have you guys started to have those conversations with OMB already around this funding?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There hasn't been a pointed conversation with OMB at this time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you're aware, the administration had to deal with a significant budget crisis, but we are having internal conversations and talking with LAC about the nature of the proposal.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The adult literacy forward initiative is currently funded by council, which was created in partnership with the expertise of providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It focuses on meeting the community needs with an innovation and holistic approach to adult literacy.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is DYCD open to adopting aspects of this program for DYCD's RFP adult literacy program?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're happy to continue to support the council's pilot.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you will recall, there was a prior iteration before adult literacy forward.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And for our part, we believe that we learned from that model as well as a pilot that we did.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's why we increased to a significant incentive the price per participant under the current literacy model.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we currently like and abide by the model that we have, and we'd be happy to continue to receive support from the councils we have in prior years to expand the number of slots made available under the model.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The next question I have is just around the Fathers Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is the current budget for the Fathers Initiative?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'll defer to Navita to answer that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Question.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And who are the providers that received the awards?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And what boroughs and districts?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Chair Stevens, I don't have all that detail.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But for the budget, I can tell you that the budget for fatherhood at FY27, sorry, is 8.2 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In terms of the providers, there are 14 providers operating 17 contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Six are in the Bronx, four in Brooklyn, three in Manhattan, two in Queens, and two in Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We can follow up with you after the hearing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, don't you worry.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You guys are going to be following up a whole lot because I still have a bunch of questions I didn't get through, and I'm trying to be really quick.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They told me I got into 215, so I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It was about these dog ones.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, here it goes.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about FY26 OmniCard spending. Bailey reports approximately $4M spent on 80,000 cards for 40,000 participants across 90 providers, with tight tracking policies for distribution and storage.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions OmniCards spending and distribution

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, so my next question is just around what is the actual spending for the Omni cards, budgeted at $11 million for the Omni cards, and how many providers have received the cards?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I should have had you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We were really trying not to do the shuffling.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It didn't work out the way we hoped.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can you repeat that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You're asking about the OmniCards.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What was the actual spending for FY26 budgeted for the $11 million for the OmniCards?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How many providers in you have received those cards?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And does the agency receive any reports from providers on a distribution of those cards?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I will let Valerie speak more to the distribution and providers and receive, but I can tell you that in last year we spent approximately $4 million towards the

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The Omni cards.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, and that funding went to about 80,000 cards.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Participants need multiple cards to get through the summer, so it was for about 40,000 participants that we purchased cards for, and they were distributed to 90 providers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we have very tight policies around the provider's responsibility of tracking which participants get the cards, how many cards they've distributed, where they store them, and they report all of that back to us on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then we use that information to take into account the need for each.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Following program cycle.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Advocates have been, and this is,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, it's 2.16.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'll stop.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, so we are short for time, and I don't want to go over it anymore, but I still have a ton of questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We'll be sending them over to you guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We want to make sure that we know that we're going into negotiations, but please do not let that hold up getting these questions because we really want to get through it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so we'll be sending them over as soon as possible, and I know there was even colleagues who have questions, so we'll be sending those over, and please make sure that we're working in tandem to get this done and not let negotiations get in the way of that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So thank you guys for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Now I'll pass it back over to Sherry Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I mean, you basically said it all.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Very efficient time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just want to thank you all again for being such great partners.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Staff quiet the chamber and remind attendees of dais and food rules before the MOIA portion begins.
chapters.title: Break and transition to MOIA hearing

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet down, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We are getting ready to begin with our next panel.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet down, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet in the chambers.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please take your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We are getting ready to begin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just a reminder, no one may approach the dais at any time during this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: There are no food or beverages allowed in this room as well.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I have to.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, everyone please find their seat.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We are getting ready to begin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seat and quiet down, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet in the chambers.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just a reminder, no one may approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No food or beverages are allowed in this room.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs.

chapters.summary: Lee welcomes Commissioner Faiza Ali and outlines MOIA's proposed FY27 budget of $781,962, unchanged from preliminary. She notes the tiny percentage of the total city budget and previews questions on AAPI services, contract delays, mental health, EO 13, and the 2030 census.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens MOIA hearing with FY27 budget overview

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's good to see familiar faces.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so good afternoon and welcome to today's third hearing about the FY27 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Linda Lee, and I'm glad to be joined today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Glad to be joined today by my colleague, Chair Elsie Encarnacion, Chair of the Committee on Immigration.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by our Deputy Speaker Williams, Councilmember Brewer, Councilmember Joseph, Councilmember Wong, and Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome to a familiar face, Commissioner Faiza Ali and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26-30 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs proposed fiscal 27 budget of $781,962 represents

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 0.00063, and I feel like that's important to note with such a sizable immigrant population in our city, but

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: of the administration's proposed FY27 budget and the executive plan, and this remains unchanged from the originally budgeted amount in the fiscal 27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, MOIA's headcount was 31.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: one less or three less, I'm sorry, than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the council's preliminary budget response, we called on the mayor to ensure the critical and essential services we provide to our immigrant population be upheld and that we safeguard programs that may be at risk with any possible federal funding cuts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And my questions are largely going to focus on a bunch of the services around the AIPA community, funding, the consolidation of all immigrant services into a single agency, of course, contracts, payment delays, which is one of our favorite topics,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: prioritizing access to mental health and health services, the legal support hotline.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: City preparing for the 2030 census and also Moyes-Roland preparing for the 26 FIFA World Cup.

chapters.summary: Encarnacion condemns the San Diego Islamic Center shooting, cites 800 NYC ICE arrests of non-targets, and urges restoration of last year's $50M+ for immigration legal services plus an additional $30M. She praises Commissioner Ali's role in securing prior funding.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnacion opening statement on immigrant protections and legal services

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Chair Encarnacion, for her opening statement.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Good afternoon to Commissioner Ali and her team.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Before we get started, I want to take a quick moment to thank the entire Council Finance Division and committee staff for their efforts in preparing for today's hearings and all of the hearings alongside the Finance Chair.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I'm sending her all of my reserved energy to continue on through this budget process.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I also want to start off by condemning the horrific shooting that occurred at the Islamic Center of San Diego on May 18th and stand in solidarity with our Muslim community.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Muslim brothers and sisters, to say hate and violence against our vibrant immigrant communities will never be tolerated.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: We offer our condolences to the families of the victims whose lives were taken.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Our immigrant communities continue to be under attack.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Last fall, we saw an uptick in street arrest by ICE, and recently the news publication The City reported that 800 New Yorkers were arrested by ICE between October 2025 and March 2026.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: And they were not even the intended targets of immigration officers.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: The federal administration has threatened to increase the so-called collateral arrest in sanctuary cities like New York.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Last year, the council fought long and hard to secure a historic level of over $50 million for immigration legal services, doubling the city's investment to safeguard immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: The Council values the partnership through task force meetings it has formed since last year with MOIA, the Office of Civil Justice, and DOE Project Open Arms in prioritizing funding and resources for our immigrant neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: For fiscal 2027, the Council is urging the restoration of last year's budget amount and an additional $30 million to further protect and enhance our city's immigration legal services in this time of great need.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I'll be repeating that number over and over again so you don't have to write it down.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: No worries.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Commissioner Ali, you played a critical role.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: in securing that funding last year.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: And we're asking you to continue your advocacy within the administration.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: We look forward to an open and honest conversation today's hearing, and I will now turn it back over to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just before we start, just a couple of housekeeping rules.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So as a reminder for this year's executive budget, because

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have a shorter timeline.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're doing all of the public testimony on one day on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please make sure to sign up for June 10th, Wednesday, 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'll try to make it as fun and exciting all day as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please come join us that day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also, because we're pressed for time, because we have to be out of the space and we only have the translators for a certain number of hours,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If members could keep their questions brief and if you guys could keep the responses as brief as possible so we can get through everything, that'd be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if we can keep the openings to 10 minutes, if it goes over, it's okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But yes, okay, awesome.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Ali and Deputy Commissioners Sanchez and Lucero before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Swearing in of MOIA commissioner and deputy commissioners

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And oh, turn over to council first, wearing it.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council members, Commissioner Ali?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Lucero, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Ali describes witnessing intimidation at 26 Federal Plaza, ICE presence at Wyckoff Medical Center, and the IRC's audit under EO 13. She emphasizes MOIA's role in restoring trust and ensuring access to services for all New Yorkers regardless of status.
chapters.title: Commissioner Ali: ICE enforcement climate and EO 13 compliance

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great, thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Good afternoon and thank you to Chair Encarnacion and members of the Committee on Immigration as well as Chair Lee, Deputy Speaker Dr.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Nantasha Williams and members of the Committee on Finance for this opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: My name is Faiza Ali and I'm the Commissioner for the New York City Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs or MOIA and I'm joined today by my colleagues Lorena Lucero, Deputy Commissioner of Programs and Policy as well as Jasnia Sanchez,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Acting Chief of Staff and Deputy Commissioner of Administration and Strategic Initiatives.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As the daughter of immigrants from Azad Kashmir, Pakistan, I understand deeply the role that local government must play in ensuring communities are treated with fairness, dignity, and respect.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Across the country, aggressive ICE enforcement operations have sown fear and uncertainty in immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And in this moment, New York City must be clear.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We remain committed to protecting all New Yorkers, regardless of their immigration status.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At a time when immigrant families are increasingly being met with disdain or contempt and hostility, local government must be a source of stability, clarity, and protection.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That requires investment, operational capacity, and sustained support to meet our responsibility to immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Today I will provide a brief update on MOIA.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and highlight six key areas of our work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Immigration Legal Services, the Immigration Legal Support Hotline, the Haitian Response Initiative, Rapid Response Collaborative, Language Justice and English Language Learning, and the Community Engagement Programs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: During a recent visit to 26 Federal Plaza, I watched immigrant New Yorkers arrive for a routine immigration proceedings only to encounter an atmosphere of intimidation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hallways were filled with families lawfully seeking status while enforcement agents moved through the building in masks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It was deeply unsettling and a stark reminder of what is at stake for the communities that we all serve.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At MOIA's preliminary budget hearing, I outlined the challenges immigrant communities are facing under the federal administration's aggressive detention and deportation campaigns.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: ICE's recent presence at Wyckoff Medical Center in Brooklyn drew community protests and a highly visible NYPD response.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Consistent with Executive Order 13's directive to continuously review agency policies and protocols,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: NYPD, in partnership with the Interagency Response Committee, will assess whether there are any updates to the policy and procedure to be made in light of that incident.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Pursuant to EO 13, the agencies have completed their initial audit of policies and protocols with support from the Interagency Response Committee on interactions with local

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: immigration enforcement and is updating its training guidelines and materials accordingly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA remains an active participant in the ongoing work of the IRC and is firmly committed to upholding and complying with local laws.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is a moment when immigrant New Yorkers need reassurance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That their local government will stand with them and protect them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani was elected in part because New Yorkers recognize the growing instability and anxiety many immigrant families face about their place in our society.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is MOIA's responsibility to help restore trust, reinforce that commitment,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and ensure that there is continued access to schools, health care, housing, and city services for all New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Ali outlines MOIA's $34.2M budget across 70+ contracts, 10 new open positions, and recent meetings with providers. She emphasizes the need for funding stability and vision so providers can deliver urgently needed services.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: FY27 budget, staffing, and provider engagement

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In the interest of time, I'm going to skip around in testimony, but you do have my full statement as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With a fiscal year 27 budget of approximately $34.2 million, Moyer maintains more than 70 contracts with nonprofit organizations serving immigrant communities across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have already communicating funding levels to all contracted providers so that they can plan accordingly for the next fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Over the past few weeks, it feels like it's been several months already at MOIA, but over the past few weeks, I have met directly with our providers to better understand how the city can be a strong community.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They are meeting this moment with urgency, compassion, and a deep community trust, doing critical work on the front lines.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Now more than ever, the city must provide stability in both funding and vision so providers can continue delivering urgently needed services to immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At the same time, MOIA is continuing to build a team capable of advancing Mayor Momdani's bold agenda.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Ten new positions at MOIA are currently open, and we expect to move forward with hiring in the coming weeks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I appreciate the Council's continued attention to MOIA's staffing levels and look forward to sharing additional updates soon.

chapters.summary: Ali details 29 legal service providers, the $18M+ investment in legal support centers launched July 2025, and 12,000+ comprehensive legal screenings conducted since launch.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: immigration legal services network and legal support centers

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Over the past year, MOIA has strengthened its legal service infrastructure during a period of dramatically increased need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA's immigration legal programs include legal support centers, the immigration legal support hotline, the legal technical mentorship program, and the rapid response legal collaborative.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Together, these initiatives expand access to trusted immigration legal support at a time when the demand continues to grow.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our immigration legal service network includes 29 providers serving communities across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Last summer, MOIA launched the Immigrant

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: legal support centers supported by more than $18 million in investments over three years to strengthen community-based organizations providing culturally and linguistically responsive immigration legal assistance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Since the program's launch in July 2025, the LSEs have conducted more than 12,000 comprehensive legal screenings, helping immigrants address a wide range of legal needs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: These investments are critical not only to expand services, but to strengthen the long-term capacity of the organizations immigrant New Yorkers rely on the most.

chapters.summary: Ali reports 19,800+ hotline calls this year, expanded hours, WhatsApp communications, 10 multilingual reporting languages, and a family preparedness phone bank with ONA that fielded 225 calls with 56 volunteers.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: hotline expansion and family preparedness

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Another critical resource for immigrant New Yorkers is MOIA's Immigration Legal Support Hotline that's operated by Catholic Charities, which helps New Yorkers connect to comprehensive immigration legal screenings and support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So far this year, the hotline has received more than 19,800 calls.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In FY26, MOIA increased funding to expand the hotline's accessibility and responsiveness as callers face increasingly urgent and complex immigration challenges.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This April, we partnered with Catholic Charities to host a family preparedness FODMANC, responding to concerns from parents and caregivers about family separation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In collaboration with the New York State Office of New Americans, the Mexican Consulate, and the New York Legal Assistance Group, 56 volunteers fielded 225 calls.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As part of it,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This expansion, the hotline increased staffing capacity, including hiring additional counselors and a staff member specifically designated to respond to time-sensitive rapid response needs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The hours of operation were also extended beyond the previously Monday through Friday 9 a.m.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To 6 p.m.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Schedule so that callers have more opportunities to reach the support outside of a standard work hour day.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The hotline also launched WhatsApp communications, expanded multilingual reporting from four to ten languages, and added three new interactive voice response language prompts.

chapters.summary: Ali describes HRI's $1.7M baselined funding supporting 7 Haitian-led CBOs and Catholic Charities, with 610 intakes this fiscal year and 6 legal clinics, amid growing Haiti instability and pending TPS Supreme Court decision.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: Haitian Response Initiative

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Moving on to the Haitian Response Initiative, MOIA coordinates HRI, an innovative program designed to support both newly arrived and longstanding Haitian residents.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The initiative funds seven Haitian-led community-based organizations to provide case management and social services, while Catholic Charities delivers immigration legal services and capacity-building trainings.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Established in FY22, HRI was baselined last year at nearly $1.7 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: HRI partners have also organized legal clinics across the city, helping hundreds of Haitian New Yorkers access legal assistance related to deportation protections, legal remedies, and work authorization.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Catholic Charities regularly conducts train-the-trainer workshops to equip community organizations

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: with real-time information needed to support residents navigating immigration-related issues independently.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This work remains especially urgent, as we know, amid growing instability in Haiti and a pending Supreme Court decision that will determine the future of TPS.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In FY26 alone, HRI has completed six legal clinics with an additional asylum application review clinic scheduled today, May 28, 2026, at Brooklyn Borough Hall.

chapters.summary: Ali details the $3M FY26 increase for rapid response legal efforts serving detained or at-risk immigrants, and coordination with Project Open Arms in NYC Public Schools for know-your-rights and direct services.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: Rapid Response Legal Collaborative and schools

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I will skip now to and talk a little bit about the Rapid Response Legal Collaborative.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA has significantly expanded our Rapid Response Legal Collaborative, which provides legal assistance to immigrant New Yorkers who are detained or at imminent risk of detention or deportation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To meet the growing demand, MOIA increased our investment in rapid response legal efforts by $3 million this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA also coordinates rapid response efforts in schools to ensure that immigrant families receive timely information and support when immigration enforcement activity occurs in or near their communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It includes connecting schools, families, and community partners to legal resources, know your rights information, and direct services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Referrals are centralized through Project Open Arms, a dedicated team within New York City Public Schools that coordinates across public schools, including charter schools, District 75, and District 79.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: nine schools as well to support immigrant families and help ensure access to a full range of services.

chapters.summary: Ali describes MOIA's oversight of 46+ agencies' language access implementation, a historic citywide convening of language access coordinators, and an in-house team providing translation/interpretation to 37 mayoral offices and agencies, including migrant center support.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: language access and in-house translation team

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I also wanted to provide an update on MOYA's language access work, an area that impacts nearly every aspect of city government.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOYA oversees and monitors and provides technical assistance to more than 46 city agencies in implementing language access services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Language justice is not simply a

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: compliance obligation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is a core equity and public trust issue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: New Yorkers cannot fully access critical services or exercise their rights or meaningfully engage with city government if language remains a barrier to information, to care, or participation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This work impacts approximately 1.8 million New Yorkers with limited English proficiency and is essential to ensuring equitable access to city services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On April 17th, MOIA convened all of the city's language access coordinators for a historic citywide meeting and launched working groups focused on strengthening relationships

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: with language service providers, multilingual strategic communication and engagement,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: bilingual talent and language access training, as well as language access in data and evaluation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In addition, MOIA has built an in-house language services team that provides translation and interpretation support to mayoral

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: offices and city hall.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thus far in FY26, the team has delivered translation and interpretation services to 37 mayoral offices and agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The LS team has also provided on-site language support at migrant centers and played a critical role in rapidly translating updated shelter protocol for recent arrivals.

chapters.summary: Ali reports 300+ immigrant rights workshop presentations reaching 7,000+ people, and 10 community/ethnic media roundtables to share critical information through hyperlocal outlets that immigrant communities trust.
chapters.title: Commissioner testimony: community engagement and ethnic media

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On community engagement, finally, I wanted to share that MOYA's community engagement work helps immigrant New Yorkers access services, understand their rights, and stay connected to trusted information during a time of

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: heightened fear and uncertainty.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Through our immigrant rights workshops, MOIA staff deliver presentations that connect community organizations and their members to free high-quality immigration legal services and know your rights resources.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In FY26, the program delivered over 300 presentations reaching over 7,000 people citywide, continuing our critical work reaching diverse communities where they are.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA also continues to strengthen relationships with trusted ethnic and community media outlets that serve immigrant New Yorkers in multiple languages.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In the past year, MOIA has led 10 community and ethnic media roundtables to share critical information through hyperlocal newspapers and television stations that immigrant communities rely on for timely and accurate information.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So far during my tenure, we have convened three such roundtables with additional sessions scheduled for this summer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In conclusion, MOYA was created to ensure that immigrant New Yorkers can fully participate in the civic, economic, and cultural life of the city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that mission is especially important and critical during periods of rapid policy change and growing uncertainty for immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Meeting this moment requires strong coordination across agencies, deep partnership with community-based organizations, and continued investment in the systems that help immigrant New Yorkers

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: access services, understand their rights, and remain connected to their neighborhoods and opportunities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As the needs facing immigrant communities continue to evolve,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA will remain focused on building trust, on strengthening that coordination, and ensuring New York City remains a place where immigrant families can live with dignity, safety, and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Finally, I want to thank the city's dedicated public servants, many who are in this room and make

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: these hearings run, and our community partners for their continued support and responding every day to the needs of both recently arrived and longstanding immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Immigrant New Yorkers are not separate from the story of this city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They are central to it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They are workers, parents, students, business owners, neighbors, and caregivers who sustain the city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: every day.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I look forward to working with the council to ensure that our policies and investments and our institutions reflect that reality while continuing to strengthen the communities that are in fact the backbone of our city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I just want to say thank you to you also, because even when you were back in the day in the council,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: when I was at KCS, you were so instrumental in helping a lot of our immigrant communities and nonprofit organizations be that bridge to us accessing folks in government.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I think it's very crucial.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's a very crucial role that you played and still play even more so now in this role.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about limitations of MOIA being a mayoral office rather than a full agency. Ali acknowledges structural limitations but notes strengths in proximity to executive decision-making, and calls for broader stakeholder conversation about potential transition.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions MOIA structure as office vs. full agency

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'll go through my questions quickly because I know we have others that need to ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So since MOIA is a mayoral office technically and not a full-fledged city agency, what specific challenges and limitations does this structure present in terms of funding, operational capacity, and policy implementation?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know you have to go across a lot of different city agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, this is a great question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think it's actually a really important conversation that should be had.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it does, you know, I think that it deserves.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: More nuanced and sort of a binary answer of like should MOIA be an agency or not.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know right now MOIA's structure as an office it does create certain limitations because we are like you said fundamentally a coordinating office rather than a standalone operational

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: agency and much of our work does require sustained partnership across city government.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But at the same time, I think that there are some real strengths to the current structure that we should take into consideration.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Being housed within City Hall, for example, gives MOIA

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the direct proximity to executive decision-making on issues related to immigrants and immigrant inclusion,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: it really allows us to also elevate our concerns as a citywide priority rather than being siloed into a standalone agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But I say all this to say that, you know, I think it's really important for me, and I'm glad you raised the issue of input and engagement with advocates.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think this is a conversation that needs to be had more broadly with other stakeholders, you know, to fully understand, you know, what will the impact be, in fact, if we were to transition into an agency or not.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there, are there regular meetings or task force groups or conversations?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm assuming the answer is yes, but if you could speak to that a little bit, because between MOIA, HRA, DYCD, SBS, DOHMH, like there's a lot of agencies I feel like that you touch, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so how has that been?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In terms of, I mean, the last few weeks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, I mean, there is a longstanding interagency task force that MOIA is responsible for.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It brings to the table all 46 city agencies for the very purpose of making sure that we're sharing updates and information, you know, that can potentially impact immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So those meetings happen quarterly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But I'll go a step further, you know, and just tap into my organizing background.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's really important for me as the leader of this office to also engage commissioners at some of the leading agencies where immigrant New Yorkers seek services and make sure that we're building across as commissioners as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I've been, you know, focused on having those meetings, having those conversations, and making sure that we're elevating those issues even outside of the interagency task force meetings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee cites Stop AAPI Hate data showing 7x increase in ICE arrests and AALDEF report gaps. Ali details partnerships with AAPI-serving organizations, challenges with lesser-known languages, and reliance on ethnic media for outreach.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions AAPI community legal services and language access gaps

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then moving now to the AAPI community support, I know that there was an analysis done by Stop AAPI Hate, and it found that a lot of the ICE arrests actually have gone up seven times in the Asian community.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then there have been over 1,400 arrests of individuals with Asian citizenship in New York compared to 205 arrests of Asian immigrants during a similar period.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in April, the ALDEF, Asian American Legal Defense Fund, released a report highlighting systemic gaps in language access, outreach, funding structure, and institutional partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How has your office or will your office increase?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: access to legal services specifically for the AAPI community and what are some challenges you've had connecting to the population to legal services which I'm sure a lot of is language and other things but

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: yeah I think first it's really important to sort of highlight that MOIA meets

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the needs of AAPI immigrant communities through sustained engagement with stakeholders and advocates, different organizations, and that includes ongoing meeting with advocates and community organizations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have close partnership with AAPI serving organizations, and many of them receive funding and run our legal support centers or our immigrant rights workshops.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that one of the biggest challenges, to answer your question more directly, is language access.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Earlier this year, MOIA met with ALDEF after they released that report, and I think one of the findings that really stood out and reflects what we see in our own work is that nearly six in ten

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: New Yorkers who speak an Asian language have limited English proficiency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And while many of the legal services hotlines right now primarily operate in English and Spanish, and so for many AAPI immigrants, the legal system can seem inaccessible, but language is only part of the challenge.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that AAPI communities is incredibly diverse as well with different immigration histories and cultural contexts and levels of familiarity, frankly, with the systems.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like, you know, typically we tend to lean on a one-size-fits-all approach to outreach, but in this instance, it just is not something that would work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There are real gaps when it comes to culturally and linguistically competent legal services, particularly for some of the lesser common languages that are spoken.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, I think that's...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Primarily a reason why MOIA has focused heavily on our partnerships, especially when we're doing our multilingual Noya Rights outreach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've relied heavily on our ethnic media engagement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, the community media is where many of the Asian communities seek, you know, trusted information from.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so that has been really important for us to forge those relationships with ethnic media and to get the information out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think this is across all communities in general and something we've heard in our roundtable conversations where there really is such a shortage of culturally competent attorneys that are available.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what are some ways that, and you sort of alluded to this with the partnership aspect, but what are some ways our local AAPI serving community-based organizations can partner with you to bridge this gap?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know there are areas where I think additional culturally and linguistically responsive legal capacity would make a real meaningful difference, especially for our AAPI communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, I think meeting those requirements.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: requires sustained investment in community-led legal infrastructures.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, one specific way that MOIA is already working on this is really through capacity building initiatives.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, I mentioned in my testimony the legal technical mentorship program that's administered by IARC, making sure that our AAPIs-led and serving organizations have access to that capacity building tool is really critical.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how many of 19,830 callers were connected to legal services. Ali clarifies no PII is collected, but legal support centers have served 12,000+ people. A targeted multilingual Know Your Rights media campaign launches mid-June in Wolof, Tibetan, Korean, Bengali and more.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions legal support hotline outreach and connection rates

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then moving to the legal support hotline, because I know that you were saying that you received 14,700 calls in FY26,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: and I know the council also supported this with an additional 500,000 in FY26 to further expand the hotline, I think, and outreach to the Asian and African communities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanted to get a sense of...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How you sort of, and you sort of mentioned this already, but how you were able to partner with ethnic media or local community-based groups to do the outreach for the hotline specifically.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'll share a little bit about the council's support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Also, you know, we appreciate the additional investment in FY26 to help just bring more awareness to the hotline and specifically do outreach to these particular communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right now, MOIA is currently finalizing a targeted multilingual NOIA Rights media campaign that's supported.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: through this additional funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We anticipate to launch that in mid-June.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The campaign is being designed specifically to reach New Yorkers, some of the more hard-to-reach New York communities, specifically in our Asian and African communities, through our ethnic media platforms.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And our outreach will be expanded to include radio and TV and print and digital media and also other platforms, frankly, that more of these communities utilize, like YouTube, Facebook, and Instagram.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The campaign, and we can share additional breakdown, but there are a number of languages that will be included in this as part of

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the outreach campaign in Wolof, in Tibetan, in Korean, in Bengali, and we can provide the council with a full list.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then how many of the total 14,700 callers were effectively connected to legal services providers, and how many of them actually received legal support, if you have that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just to clarify on the numbers, from July 1, 2025 to May 15, 2026, the hotline received 19,000 calls.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: When a caller calls the hotline, we do not collect PII information, so personal identifying information.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We do direct them to our legal support centers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Overall, the legal support centers have served over 12,000 people.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and okay, so, but that doesn't just include the legal services, but maybe referring them to different community organizations for other types of support, or is this specifically just for the legal?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The hotline itself provides referrals to primarily our legal support centers for legal services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's sort of the why it was created, but it also provides connections to other agencies and things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee cites a provider owed $15M and spending thousands on loan interest. Ali strongly supports continued investment in contract processing capacity, notes recent procurement reform laws, and describes close partnership with MOX and the Mayor's Office of Nonprofit Services.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions contract and payment delays for immigration legal providers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then let me just get back to Sanor.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For contract and payment delays, moving on a little bit, we know that there's, you guys are, you know, the legal services groups are dealing with an unprecedented attack on immigrants'rights.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And one of the biggest issues that providers expressed during our roundtable that we had was the extreme delays in getting paid by the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One provider explained that the city owes them $15 million, and they have now spent thousands of dollars on interest payments from private loans just to make payroll.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is a common sort of theme that we hear amongst our nonprofit providers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wondering how, Moya, if you can advocate for this administration to dedicate more funding to processing the contracts and talking to MOX,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: ensuring that providers have timely access to invoicing and reimbursements.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can start and then I'll turn it over to my colleague if she has anything more to add.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, this is an area that you all know I care about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: very deeply about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I did during my time at the council.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA strongly supports continued investment in contract processing capacity and reforms to improve payment timelines for our providers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We recognize that a lot of our immigrant-serving organizations are operating under significant financial pressure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think it's important for me to just kind of elevate that last session the council did enact major procurement reforms, including laws establishing advanced payments and quarterly invoicing and stronger accountability.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Those reforms were really an important step forward, but implementation across the agencies is something that's critical.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're continuing to partner with our agencies and talking to MOX and providers to identify any operational bottlenecks and figure out ways to...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: together, how do we improve that consistency and transparency?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I will just add that, at least on the MOIA end, you know, we have a close partnership with both MOX and the Mayor's Office of Nonprofit Services, who are

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: a great resource for all nonprofits, especially folks that are new to, you know, the invoicing or contracting process over, you know, here in the city, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we definitely, at least when things get elevated to us, we definitely work closely with providers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To point them in the right direction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think you answered my question, Commissioner, already about, I think HR and DSS are probably some of the ones that we hear most about from the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so whatever you guys can do to help push those contracts through would be, I think, immensely helpful.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about post-detention mental health services. Ali describes coordination with DOHMH and Envision Freedom Fund, and preliminary research to assess mental health impacts and identify service gaps post-detention.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions mental health support for post-detention immigrants

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and moving on to health and mental health, how, so I know that aside from the traditional

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: mental health and health services, I know that more specifically there's been a lot of trauma around the federal immigration detention that's been happening across the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you support immigrants post-detention with their mental health needs or other services, and what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA has been working really closely with agency partners, including DOHMH, to really understand better the kinds of support and mental health support that we can provide to immigrants following detention.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This includes coordination with organizations such as the Envision Freedom Fund, which is a bail and bond support nonprofit organization to strengthen connections between community-based organizations and the relevant agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: MOIA also, I'm proud to say, is in the preliminary stages of research to assess the mental health impacts that immigration post-detention and to identify

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Where are the gaps in services and support that is needed?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we'll continue to engage our partners on this process and share those additional findings once we have them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about planning for potential federal clawbacks. Ali confirms legal services are city-funded and shielded from direct clawbacks, but MOIA actively tracks trickle-down effects and federal policy changes including HUD mixed-status rule proposals.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions preparation for federal funding cuts and clawbacks

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will there be, I mean, given that we're looking at federal cuts that are obviously going to hurt the refugee asylum seeker services, have you been having conversations with the administration, OMB?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know if there's any additional funding for immigration services in the executive plan that go towards specifically funding wraparound services for this population?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The administration continues to evaluate, you know, community needs across legal services and case management and other stabilization support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, at MOIA, we strongly recognize the importance of holistic and trauma-informed approaches, particularly for vulnerable populations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so while there may not be additional funding in the executive plan for wraparound immigration services, we do want to take a moment to highlight again the work of the Haitian Response

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Initiative because they have done incredible work in this area.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we have specific data, but the HRI has completed about 610 intakes so far this fiscal year and has completed a certain number of trainings as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there is an example of folks within MOIA's network where they have

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: offered and do offer this trauma-informed care.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And two things really quickly I just want to uplift is that during our roundtable discussions with a lot of the providers,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: definitely ways to retain immigration attorneys and if there's anything you all can do on your side to really emphasize to admin, OMB, that we need extra funds to make sure that we're able to retain the attorneys

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: with higher salaries and pay, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then of course, purpose of funds, making sure that the funding can be flexible because there's a lot of different types of services that they need to provide.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so those are two things I just wanted to highlight from our roundtable conversations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm going to pause there and I have a couple questions, but let me move on to my co-chair, Chair Encarnación.

chapters.summary: Encarnacion presses for clarity on MOIA's 10 open positions housed across mayor's office, DCAS, DSS, and SBS. Ali confirms all staff execute MOIA's mission regardless of payroll location, with a total PS budget of approximately $5.3M across agencies.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnacion questions MOIA budget, headcount, and staff reporting structure

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Hello again.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: So I'm going to jump right in because we are pressed for time.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: But I wanted to talk about the budget, MOIA's budget and your headcount.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: You know, it's always challenging to try to conduct oversight because of the programs.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: They're spread throughout multiple agencies.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: That was already discussed.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I know that you highlighted some strengths and some challenges that we may have in calling for an independent agency.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I'm going to continue to call for that because I really feel that there are some major wins that we can get there.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: But I appreciate the want and need for further communication around that.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: In your testimony, you mentioned 10 new positions at MOIA that they are currently open.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Under which agencies are those 10 new positions housed?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: And does that include the three vacant positions that we had spoke about during the prelim budget?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Or is it in addition to those three?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, that does include those three vacancies that we spoke about in the prelim.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And these vacancies are spread through the mayor's office, DCAS, DSS, and SBS.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: What was the first one before DCAS?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: DSS.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: The mayor's office, okay.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: And this is all still with the same baseline budget of $781.962.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, so our lines overall, they're spread throughout, you know, they're housed at different agencies over at the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
